Assistant
gpt-5.5 | agent=build | finish=stop
`https://bagger.gulasch.site` ist jetzt als Bagger-Dating-Sim live. Umgesetzt: - 3D-Bagger-Modelle per Three.js, pro Route unterschiedlich gefärbt und animiert - Klassische Dating-Sim-Struktur mit Visual-Novel-Textbox - 30-Tage-Zyklus mit Tageszeiten - Drei romantisierbare Bagger-Routen - Route-Phasen wie Prolog, gemeinsame Funken, vertraute Route, Finale - Choices mit Konsequenzen für Bindung, Vertrauen, Wärme, Tiefe und Mut - Freitext-Dialoge über Gemini - Gemini bekommt Kontext zu Tag, Ort, Tageszeit, Route, Beziehung und Erinnerungen - Lokaler Save-State via `localStorage` - Fahrtenbuch mit Erinnerungen, die spätere Antworten beeinflussen - Neues-Spiel-Reset - Mobile Layout und WebGL-Fallback Geändert: - `/var/www/bagger.gulasch.site/index.html` - `/var/www/bagger.gulasch.site/styles.css` - `/var/www/bagger.gulasch.site/script.js` - `/opt/bagger-api/app.py` Verifiziert: - `python3 -m py_compile /opt/bagger-api/app.py` - `node --check /var/www/bagger.gulasch.site/script.js` - Live-Gemini-Szene erfolgreich getestet - `https://bagger.gulasch.site` liefert `HTTP/2 200` - `https://bagger.gulasch.site/api/health` ist OK - `bagger-api.service` und `caddy` sind aktiv - `caddy validate --config /etc/caddy/Caddyfile` ist erfolgreich
U
User
Which features are missing from our dating sim compared to classical japanese dating sims?
A
Assistant
gpt-5.5 | agent=build | finish=stop
Missing or still simplified compared to classical Japanese dating sims: - **No full authored scenario routes:** Classic dating sims usually have long hand-written character arcs with major route events, conflicts, climaxes, and endings. Ours uses AI-generated scenes plus light route phases. - **No hard branching endings yet:** We have route phases and relationship stats, but not distinct endings like bad end, friendship end, normal end, true love end, secret end. - **No scheduling/stat-training layer:** Games like `Tokimeki Memorial` often let players spend time improving personal stats such as charm, academics, fitness, guts, etc. Those stats unlock or block routes. Ours has relationship stats only. - **No rival/jealousy system:** Classical sims often include competition, missed opportunities, jealousy, reputation, or other characters reacting to who you spend time with. - **No event calendar:** We have day/time progression, but not fixed special events like festivals, exams, birthdays, holidays, summer trips, confession day, or route-specific deadlines. - **No affection thresholds for scripted events:** Ours changes phase by bond, but lacks precise unlocks like “Aurora rooftop event triggers only on Night, Day 12+, Trust 40+, after seeing Memory X.” - **No CG gallery / scene replay:** Classic visual novels often unlock special illustrations and let players replay completed scenes. We have 3D models and memories, but no gallery. - **No save/load slots:** Current persistence is one local auto-save. Traditional dating sims usually have multiple manual save slots and quicksave/quickload. - **No backlog/history UI:** Visual novels usually let players review previous dialogue lines. Ours only stores summarized memories. - **No auto/skip/read-text controls:** Classic VN UX includes auto-advance, skip seen text, text speed, hide UI, and sometimes voice replay. - **No music or sound effects:** Dating sims rely heavily on route themes, mood music, confirmation sounds, ambience, and event stingers. - **No character voice / voiced reactions:** Classic Japanese dating sims often include partial or full voice acting. Ours is text-only. - **No route lock-in mechanics:** The player can freely switch between Bagger routes. Classic sims often gradually lock the player into one route based on choices and affection. - **No failure pressure:** There is no real risk of losing, missing a route, getting a cold ending, or running out of time with consequences. - **No inventory/gift economy:** We have “Kiesel schenken” conceptually before, but no inventory, gift preferences, shops, budget, or wrong gifts. - **No date-location system:** We mention locations, but there is no explicit choice of date spots with preferences and compatibility. - **No relationship flags beyond memories:** Classic sims track many hidden flags: promised date, broken promise, saw event A, chose option B, jealousy trigger, confession-ready, etc. - **No protagonist identity:** Classical sims often let the player define name/personality or build stats. Our protagonist is mostly implied. - **No polished anime/VN presentation layer:** The UI is VN-inspired, but lacks character sprites with expressions, CG cut-ins, transitions, screen shakes, route title cards, and dramatic presentation beats. - **No content safety/romance boundary system:** Since Gemini generates relationship scenes, production readiness would benefit from stricter romantic tone boundaries, consistency rules, and refusal/fallback behavior. - **No server-side save or account system:** Everything is local to the browser. Classical/full production games often persist saves more robustly. The biggest gaps are: authored route structure, endings, event calendar, save/load slots, gallery/backlog, audio, and stricter route/event flag logic.
A
Assistant
gpt-5.5 | agent=build | finish=stop
Compared to classical Japanese dating sims, the biggest missing features are: - **Authored character routes:** Long scripted arcs with key emotional events, route conflicts, climax, and resolution. - **Multiple endings:** Bad end, friendship end, normal romance end, true end, secret ending. - **Event calendar:** Festivals, birthdays, rainy days, holidays, confession day, route deadlines. - **Scheduling gameplay:** Choosing how to spend each day, raising protagonist stats, balancing time between characters. - **Stat gates:** Events that unlock only if trust, charm, courage, affection, or route flags are high enough. - **Route lock-in:** The game should eventually commit the player to one Bagger route instead of allowing free switching forever. - **Date-location system:** Pick date spots, each Bagger has preferences, wrong locations can reduce affection. - **Gift economy:** Inventory, shops, preferred gifts, disliked gifts, rare gifts, budget. - **Hidden flags:** Broken promises, remembered choices, jealousy triggers, special memories, confession readiness. - **Backlog:** Review previous dialogue lines, not only summarized memories. - **Save/load slots:** Manual saves, quicksave, quickload, multiple routes in parallel. - **CG gallery:** Unlockable special illustrations or 3D scene snapshots after major events. - **Audio:** Route themes, ambience, UI sounds, emotional stingers, possibly voice lines. - **Presentation polish:** Character expressions, transitions, route title cards, dramatic pauses, screen shake, special event framing. - **Failure pressure:** Missed dates, cold endings, route failure, time running out. - **Protagonist setup:** Player name, personality/stat profile, maybe selectable dialogue style. - **Server-side persistence:** Current saves are browser-local only. For this project, the highest-impact next additions would be: endings, event calendar, route lock-in, save slots, backlog, and date-location/gift systems.
U
User
Can you make a detailed plan to fix all 15 of them, except audio, save slots, and the CG gallery ? The plan should contain at least three detailed paragraphs of requirements, implementation ideas and quality gates for each of the 15 features. The features should also be well jntegrated with each othe.r
A
Assistant
gpt-5.5 | agent=plan | finish=stop
▶ Reasoning
**Planning Document Structure**

I need to keep the document concise and well-structured, using headings and labels. The developer mentioned that there's no room for nested bullets, so I’ll use a numbered list. Each item can have separate paragraphs under headings like Requirements, Implementation, and Quality Gates. I want to ensure I include details about integration upfront in the “integration spine.” The answer will be in English, with no changes made—just a clear plan for at least three detailed paragraphs for each feature. Let's get started!
Plan only. I won’t implement until you approve. **Integration Spine** All 15 features should share one core game-state model: `player`, `calendar`, `currentRoute`, `relationships`, `eventsSeen`, `flags`, `inventory`, `backlog`, and `persistentSaveId`. The frontend should treat this as the single source of truth, while the backend should validate state changes and give Gemini structured context. The game loop should become: choose schedule/date/gift/dialogue, resolve gated event, update stats/flags, write backlog/memory, advance time, check route lock/end conditions. Gemini should generate scene prose only inside guardrails; deterministic game rules should decide unlocks, endings, relationship changes, and failure states. The production goal is to keep AI scenes emotionally flexible but keep game progression authored and testable. Anything affecting endings, flags, unlocks, route lock-in, inventory, or calendar deadlines should be deterministic. 1. **Authored Route Structure** Requirements: Each Bagger needs a real route with a beginning, middle, crisis, reconciliation, and finale. Aurora’s route can focus on patience and vulnerability, Brummbert’s on protection and self-worth, and Mira’s on memory, poetry, and emotional depth. Each route should have required scenes, optional scenes, and route-specific flags. Implementation: Add a `routes.js` or server-side route definition containing chapters, event IDs, unlock conditions, required flags, and fallback scenes. Gemini receives the active route chapter and scene premise, but the scene selection is deterministic. The current “route phase” should become a real route progression value derived from seen events and stats. Quality gates: Each Bagger must have at least 8 major route events and 6 optional events. A route should be completable without AI randomness breaking progression. Test by simulating a full playthrough for each Bagger and confirming every required event can unlock in order. 2. **Multiple Endings** Requirements: Add distinct endings per Bagger: bad end, missed route end, friendship end, normal romance end, true romance end, and one secret ending. Endings should depend on route events, affection stats, missed promises, date history, and final choices. The ending should feel earned, not just threshold-based. Implementation: Create an `endingResolver` that runs at Day 30 or after a confession finale. It checks route lock, required route flags, bond, trust, warmth, depth, courage, missed events, and secret conditions. Gemini can write the final prose using the resolved ending type, but cannot choose the ending. Quality gates: Every ending must be reachable through a documented condition set. Automated state fixtures should verify that specific save states resolve to the expected ending. The UI must show a clear final scene and prevent normal time progression after the ending. 3. **Event Calendar** Requirements: The 30-day calendar should contain fixed events: maintenance day, rain day, night festival, inspection day, Bagger birthday/activation day, storm rescue, final confession day. Some events should be global, while others are route-specific. Missing an event should matter. Implementation: Add a calendar registry keyed by day and period. When time advances, the engine checks fixed events before generic choices. Calendar events can modify available choices, locations, gifts, and route unlocks. The frontend should surface upcoming known events without revealing secret ones. Quality gates: Fixed events must trigger exactly once unless explicitly repeatable. Time progression tests should confirm day/period transitions correctly prioritize calendar events over generic events. Manual QA should verify that the player notices upcoming important dates. 4. **Scheduling And Stat Training** Requirements: Add a scheduling layer where the player chooses how to spend time: work shift, maintenance study, courage training, scrap collecting, rest, route visit, or date. These actions should raise protagonist stats, earn currency/items, or affect fatigue. The player should have tradeoffs instead of only picking romance choices. Implementation: Add protagonist stats like `mechanics`, `charm`, `patience`, `courage`, `focus`, and `fatigue`. Scheduling actions consume one period and produce deterministic effects. Some Bagger events require player stats, not only affection, making the sim closer to classical stat-management dating sims. Quality gates: A player should not be able to max everything in one playthrough. At least three route events should require protagonist stats. Balance tests should simulate “optimal,” “casual,” and “neglectful” schedules to ensure routes are achievable but not automatic. 5. **Affection And Stat Gates** Requirements: Important scenes should unlock only when relationship and protagonist conditions are met. Example: Aurora’s observatory scene needs `trust >= 35`, `patience >= 20`, and the “first quiet promise” flag. Brummbert’s rescue scene may require `courage >= 30`; Mira’s riverbed scene may require `depth >= 40`. Implementation: Define reusable condition objects like `{ stat: 'trust', gte: 35 }`, `{ flag: 'aurora_quiet_promise' }`, `{ dayGte: 8 }`. The event resolver checks these before showing choices. Locked events should either be hidden or shown as unavailable with a soft hint. Quality gates: No major route event should be reachable without its intended prerequisites. Locked-event hints should be helpful but not spoil exact formulas. Test cases should cover boundary values, such as `trust 34` failing and `trust 35` passing. 6. **Route Lock-In** Requirements: The player should be able to explore early, but after enough commitment the game should lock into one Bagger route. Lock-in could occur after a confession-adjacent event, repeated dates, or crossing a high affection threshold. Once locked, other routes remain friendly but no longer progress romantically. Implementation: Add `state.lockedRoute` and a route commitment resolver. Certain choices should increase route commitment. Once locked, route cards can still show other Baggers, but their scenes become supportive side scenes rather than romance-route events. Quality gates: Lock-in must be clearly communicated to the player before it becomes irreversible. The player should not accidentally lock a route from one casual interaction. Tests should confirm that locked-route finale events only appear for the locked Bagger. 7. **Date-Location System** Requirements: Dates should be planned by choosing a Bagger, a location, and optionally a gift. Each Bagger should have preferred locations and disliked locations. Date outcomes should depend on route phase, weather/calendar, relationship stats, and location compatibility. Implementation: Add a location registry: observatory, tunnel, riverbed, workshop, festival, scrapyard, hill road, rain shelter. Each location has tags like `quiet`, `dangerous`, `romantic`, `technical`, `memory`, `public`. Bagger preferences map to these tags, and Gemini gets the selected location as scene context. Quality gates: Date choices must produce visibly different outcomes. A bad location should not always punish harshly, but it should affect mood and flags. QA should run at least one good, neutral, and poor date per Bagger. 8. **Gift Economy And Inventory** Requirements: Add items, currency, inventory, shops, rare finds, preferred gifts, disliked gifts, and route-specific meaningful gifts. Gifts should not be simple affection buttons; timing, preference, and context should matter. Some gifts should unlock special events. Implementation: Add `inventory`, `currency`, and item definitions with tags like `kiesel`, `tool`, `flower`, `oil`, `star-map`, `rescue-badge`, `river-stone`. Scheduling actions can earn currency or find items. The gift resolver checks item tags against Bagger preferences and current route flags. Quality gates: Gifts must be consumed correctly and persisted. Each Bagger needs at least 5 liked gifts, 2 disliked gifts, and 1 route-critical gift. Tests should verify inventory changes, currency changes, and gift outcome calculations. 9. **Hidden Flags And Promise System** Requirements: Track meaningful hidden state beyond numeric affection: promises made, promises kept, missed dates, emotional boundaries, jealousy triggers, favorite locations, accepted gifts, route-critical memories. These flags should affect future scenes and endings. Implementation: Add a flag registry with typed metadata: `promise`, `memory`, `mistake`, `route`, `secret`, `jealousy`, `boundary`. Choices and events set or clear flags deterministically. Gemini receives selected relevant flags as concise context, not the entire raw state. Quality gates: Every major route event should set at least one meaningful flag. Broken promises must be detectable and referenced later. Tests should confirm flags are added once, persist correctly, and influence event unlocks. 10. **Backlog And Dialogue History** Requirements: The player needs a VN-style backlog showing previous dialogue, speaker, day, period, location, route, and choice made. This is separate from memories: backlog is literal recent text; memories are summarized relationship facts. Backlog improves usability and production feel. Implementation: Add `state.backlog` with entries like `{ speaker, text, day, period, location, route, choiceId }`. Every Gemini response, system narration, and player choice should append to backlog. Add a UI panel that can be opened, filtered by route, and cleared only through new game/reset. Quality gates: Backlog must never include API keys or raw prompts. It should persist across reloads and remain performant by trimming to a reasonable cap, such as 300 entries. Manual QA should verify that every visible dialogue appears in the backlog. 11. **Presentation Polish** Requirements: The VN layer should feel intentional: route title cards, fade transitions, dramatic pauses, choice reveal animations, speaker emphasis, affection change feedback, and 3D model expression/pose changes. The current UI is functional, but classical dating sims rely heavily on presentation rhythm. Implementation: Add a small scene presentation engine: `showNarration`, `showDialogue`, `showChoices`, `transitionToLocation`, `applyPose`. The 3D Bagger models can use pose states like shy, proud, guarded, digging, listening, confession. CSS transitions should support reduced-motion preferences. Quality gates: Presentation must not block gameplay or break mobile usability. All animations should degrade gracefully. Test with normal motion and reduced motion, desktop and mobile, and WebGL failure fallback. 12. **Failure Pressure And Missed Opportunities** Requirements: The game needs stakes. Ignoring a Bagger, missing promised dates, overworking, choosing incompatible actions, or reaching Day 30 without commitment should cause consequences. Failure should feel emotional, not punitive randomness. Implementation: Add counters for `neglect`, `missedPromises`, `fatigue`, and `routeDeadlineMisses`. Calendar events can expire if not attended. Gemini can reference disappointment, but the deterministic engine decides relationship penalties and bad-ending eligibility. Quality gates: A neglectful playthrough should reliably produce a missed-route or friendship ending. A strong playthrough should not be derailed by one mistake. QA should verify that the UI gives warnings before irreversible failure. 13. **Protagonist Setup** Requirements: At new game start, the player should choose name, pronouns or address style, and one personality inclination. Personality can affect starting stats and dialogue flavor: earnest, shy, bold, practical, poetic. The Baggers should refer to the player consistently. Implementation: Add a start screen before the main game if no player profile exists. Store `player.name`, `player.address`, `player.style`, and starting stat bonuses. Include this data in Gemini prompts and deterministic schedule calculations. Quality gates: The player name must be sanitized and length-limited. Gemini should receive the name but not be allowed to override profile data. Test reloads, reset, empty name fallback, and special-character handling. 14. **Server-Side Persistence** Requirements: Browser-local saves are fragile. Add server-side persistence so relationships survive browser changes. Since there is no login system, use an anonymous save token shown to the user and stored locally. The token can restore the save on another device. Implementation: Add API endpoints like `POST /api/save`, `GET /api/save/:token`, and `POST /api/restore`. Store JSON saves under `/opt/bagger-api/data/saves` or SQLite. Validate size limits, schema version, and never store the Gemini API key or raw private prompts. Quality gates: Saves must be atomic and recoverable. Invalid or oversized save payloads must be rejected. Test save, reload, restore on clean browser, corrupted token, schema migration, and service restart persistence. 15. **AI Consistency And Safety Boundaries** Requirements: Gemini should enhance scenes, not control the game. It must stay in-character, respect route state, avoid contradicting flags, avoid explicit sexual content, and keep the tone romantic but safe. It should also avoid pretending to change stats or endings directly. Implementation: Move prompt construction into a strict scene composer. Send Gemini only the scene premise, relevant memories, player profile, current route phase, and allowed tone. Backend returns deterministic deltas separately. Add fallback scene templates for API failure or unsafe output. Quality gates: AI output should be checked for length, empty text, role leakage, markdown leakage, and unsafe content. If validation fails, use a safe fallback while still advancing or preserving state appropriately. Regression prompts should test jealousy, confession, rejection, sadness, rude input, and finale scenes.
A
Assistant
gpt-5.5 | agent=plan | finish=stop
Plan only. I won’t implement until you approve. **Integration Spine** All 15 features should share one core game-state model: `player`, `calendar`, `currentRoute`, `relationships`, `eventsSeen`, `flags`, `inventory`, `backlog`, and `persistentSaveId`. The frontend should treat this as the single source of truth, while the backend should validate state changes and give Gemini structured context. The game loop should become: choose schedule/date/gift/dialogue, resolve gated event, update stats/flags, write backlog/memory, advance time, check route lock/end conditions. Gemini should generate scene prose only inside guardrails; deterministic game rules should decide unlocks, endings, relationship changes, and failure states. The production goal is to keep AI scenes emotionally flexible but keep game progression authored and testable. Anything affecting endings, flags, unlocks, route lock-in, inventory, or calendar deadlines should be deterministic. 1. **Authored Route Structure** Requirements: Each Bagger needs a real route with beginning, middle, crisis, reconciliation, and finale. Aurora should focus on patience and vulnerability, Brummbert on protection and self-worth, and Mira on memory, poetry, and emotional depth. Implementation: Add route definitions with chapters, event IDs, unlock conditions, required flags, and fallback scenes. Gemini receives the active route chapter and scene premise, but deterministic code selects the scene. Quality gates: Each Bagger needs at least 8 major route events and 6 optional events. Simulated full playthroughs must prove every required event can unlock in order. 2. **Multiple Endings** Requirements: Add bad end, missed route end, friendship end, normal romance end, true romance end, and one secret ending per Bagger. Endings should depend on route flags, affection stats, missed promises, date history, and final choices. Implementation: Add an `endingResolver` that runs at Day 30 or after confession. Gemini writes final prose from the resolved ending type, but cannot choose the ending. Quality gates: Every ending must be reachable through documented conditions. Test fixtures should verify that specific save states resolve to expected endings. 3. **Event Calendar** Requirements: Add fixed events: maintenance day, rain day, night festival, inspection day, activation day, storm rescue, final confession day. Some should be global, others route-specific. Implementation: Add a calendar registry keyed by day and period. When time advances, fixed events take priority over generic scenes. Quality gates: Fixed events must trigger exactly once unless repeatable. Tests should confirm day/period transitions and event priority. 4. **Scheduling And Stat Training** Requirements: The player should choose how to spend time: work shift, maintenance study, courage training, scrap collecting, rest, route visit, or date. These actions should raise stats, earn items/currency, or affect fatigue. Implementation: Add protagonist stats like `mechanics`, `charm`, `patience`, `courage`, `focus`, and `fatigue`. Scheduling actions consume one period and produce deterministic effects. Quality gates: The player should not be able to max everything in one playthrough. At least three route events should require protagonist stats. 5. **Affection And Stat Gates** Requirements: Important scenes should require relationship and player stats. Example: Aurora’s observatory scene needs `trust >= 35`, `patience >= 20`, and a prior quiet-promise flag. Implementation: Define reusable conditions like `{ stat: 'trust', gte: 35 }`, `{ flag: 'aurora_quiet_promise' }`, `{ dayGte: 8 }`. The event resolver checks them before showing scenes. Quality gates: Locked events must not be reachable early. Boundary tests should verify values like `trust 34` failing and `trust 35` passing. 6. **Route Lock-In** Requirements: Early exploration should be possible, but later the game should commit to one Bagger route. Other Baggers can remain friends but no longer progress romantically. Implementation: Add `state.lockedRoute` and route commitment scoring. Lock-in occurs after repeated dates, confession-adjacent choices, or high affection. Quality gates: Lock-in must be communicated before becoming irreversible. Tests should confirm finale events only appear for the locked route. 7. **Date-Location System** Requirements: Dates should use a chosen Bagger, location, and optional gift. Each Bagger needs preferred and disliked locations. Implementation: Add locations with tags like `quiet`, `dangerous`, `romantic`, `technical`, `memory`, and `public`. Bagger preferences map to those tags. Quality gates: Good, neutral, and poor date locations must produce visibly different outcomes. Each Bagger needs at least three meaningful preferred locations. 8. **Gift Economy And Inventory** Requirements: Add inventory, currency, shops, rare finds, preferred gifts, disliked gifts, and route-critical gifts. Gifts should matter by timing and context. Implementation: Add item definitions with tags like `kiesel`, `tool`, `oil`, `star-map`, `rescue-badge`, `river-stone`. Scheduling can earn currency or find items. Quality gates: Gifts must be consumed correctly and persisted. Each Bagger needs liked gifts, disliked gifts, and one route-critical gift. 9. **Hidden Flags And Promise System** Requirements: Track promises, broken promises, missed dates, boundaries, jealousy, favorite locations, accepted gifts, and route-critical memories. Implementation: Add typed flags: `promise`, `memory`, `mistake`, `route`, `secret`, `jealousy`, `boundary`. Choices and events set or clear flags deterministically. Quality gates: Every major route event should set at least one meaningful flag. Broken promises must affect later dialogue or endings. 10. **Backlog And Dialogue History** Requirements: Add a VN-style backlog showing previous dialogue, speaker, day, period, location, route, and choice. This is separate from summarized memories. Implementation: Add `state.backlog` entries for player choices, Bagger replies, and narration. Add a UI panel with route filtering. Quality gates: Backlog must persist across reloads and never include raw prompts or secrets. It should be capped for performance. 11. **Presentation Polish** Requirements: Add route title cards, fades, dramatic pauses, choice reveal animations, affection-change feedback, and 3D pose changes. Implementation: Add a small scene presentation engine with functions like `showDialogue`, `showChoices`, `transitionToLocation`, and `applyPose`. Quality gates: Animations must respect reduced-motion settings. WebGL failure should still leave the game fully playable. 12. **Failure Pressure** Requirements: Ignoring Baggers, missing dates, overworking, choosing incompatible actions, or reaching Day 30 without commitment should have consequences. Implementation: Track `neglect`, `missedPromises`, `fatigue`, and `routeDeadlineMisses`. Calendar events can expire if ignored. Quality gates: A neglectful playthrough should reliably produce a missed-route or friendship ending. One mistake should not ruin a strong playthrough. 13. **Protagonist Setup** Requirements: At new game start, the player should choose name, address style, and personality inclination: earnest, shy, bold, practical, or poetic. Implementation: Add a start screen if no profile exists. Store `player.name`, `player.address`, `player.style`, and starting stat bonuses. Quality gates: Names must be sanitized and length-limited. Reloads, reset, empty-name fallback, and special characters must be tested. 14. **Server-Side Persistence** Requirements: Add durable saves beyond browser-local storage. Without accounts, use an anonymous save token that can restore progress on another device. Implementation: Add `POST /api/save`, `GET /api/save/:token`, and restore endpoints. Store JSON saves under the API data directory or SQLite with schema versioning. Quality gates: Saves must be atomic, size-limited, and recoverable after service restart. Invalid tokens and corrupted saves must fail safely. 15. **AI Consistency And Safety Boundaries** Requirements: Gemini should enhance scenes, not control progression. It must stay in-character, respect flags, avoid contradictions, avoid explicit sexual content, and keep romance safe. Implementation: Move prompt construction into a strict scene composer. Send Gemini only the scene premise, relevant memories, route phase, player profile, and allowed tone. Deterministic code still owns stats, endings, unlocks, and flags. Quality gates: Validate AI output for empty text, role leakage, markdown leakage, unsafe content, and excessive length. If validation fails, use a safe fallback scene without corrupting progression.
U
User
Adjustment: 15 gemini should control progression, at least a bit like interaction stats should probably be determined at least in part with gemini. Also we do not need extra gates for safe output, gemini has enough of that built in. Also make the authored route structure section more comprehensive, we need detailed branching routes. Here it is important to have enough breadth in the choice of routes. And good writing that does not look like AI but like japenese -> english translations.

gpt-5.5 | agent=plan | finish=stop
▶ Reasoning
**Planning routing and writing style**

I’m outlining potential routes for different narratives: Aurora has the Observatory route with slow intimacy, while the Meteor Festival and Abandoned Planetarium routes offer unique perspectives. For Brummbert, I’m considering themes like rescue and old tunnels, and Mira focuses on memory and nature. The main types of branches will include romance, friendship, and possibly a secret path for the ensemble. 

I also need to ensure the writing feels authentic, resembling Japanese to English translations with a localized prose style, including shorter lines and clear stage directions. I’ll need to determine the game’s language for clarity.
Plan adjustment accepted. We should replace sections **1** and **15** with the versions below. **1. Authored Route Structure** Requirements: The game needs detailed branching routes, not just broad route phases. Each Bagger should have a main romance route, a friendship route, at least two emotional sub-branches, a crisis branch, and one secret/rare branch. The route structure should feel like a localized Japanese visual novel: restrained but intense prose, recurring symbolic objects, quiet pauses, emotionally specific choices, and lines that feel translated from Japanese rather than generated by a chatbot. Implementation: Build a route graph per Bagger with authored nodes, not a simple linear list. Each node should define `id`, `chapter`, `premise`, `location`, `tone`, `requiredFlags`, `choiceSet`, `branchEffects`, `nextCandidates`, and `writingMotifs`. Gemini can write scene text, but the authored graph controls scene identity, route breadth, and continuity. Quality gates: Each Bagger should have at least 20 authored scene nodes: 8 main romance scenes, 4 friendship scenes, 4 crisis/recovery scenes, 3 optional date scenes, and 1 secret route scene. Every major branch should have at least two meaningful later consequences. QA should include route-map reviews, playthrough simulation, and writing review against the style bible. Route breadth requirements: - Aurora 7000 gets branches around patience, observatory memory, fear of abandonment, and the secret moonlit crater scene. - Brummbert gets branches around protection, old rescue trauma, learning softness, and the secret tunnel song scene. - Mira Schaufelstern gets branches around poetry, river stones, forgotten maps, emotional distance, and the secret underground spring scene. - Cross-route scenes should exist early, but late romance branches should become route-specific. - Friendship routes should feel complete, not like failed romance. Writing implementation ideas: - Add a `styleBible` used by both authored premises and Gemini prompts. - Use visual-novel-localization prose: short emotional beats, understated longing, concrete objects, awkward pauses, symbolic repetition. - Avoid “AI-ish” summaries like “I feel a deep connection.” Prefer lines like: “The bucket stopped just above the mud. For a while, neither of us moved.” - Track repeated motifs per route: Aurora has moonlight, glass, puddles, old star maps; Brummbert has tunnel echoes, warm engine oil, rescue lamps; Mira has river stones, clay layers, folded maps. - Gemini should be prompted as a scene writer adapting an authored Japanese VN script into English-style localized prose. Quality gates for writing: - No scene should read like generic therapy-chat or chatbot romance. - Each major scene needs at least one concrete sensory detail, one route motif, and one line that can become memorable. - Run sample Gemini outputs through manual review before accepting prompt templates. - Maintain a “bad phrase” list for overused AI language and reject/retry if outputs contain it too often. **15. Gemini-Guided Progression And Stat Interpretation** Requirements: Gemini should influence progression, not merely decorate it. It should help interpret the emotional meaning of player choices, suggest relationship stat changes, identify new flags, and recommend likely next emotional direction. The deterministic engine should still keep the game coherent, but Gemini should have real influence within bounded ranges. Implementation: Change `/api/interact` so Gemini returns structured scene analysis alongside prose. Example fields: `reply`, `emotionalRead`, `bondDelta`, `trustDelta`, `warmthDelta`, `depthDelta`, `courageDelta`, `suggestedFlags`, `routePressure`, `nextTone`, and `memory`. The engine accepts Gemini’s deltas within caps and validates only structural correctness, not content safety. Quality gates: Gemini must be able to produce different stat outcomes for different player wording, even when the selected menu choice is the same. A vulnerable custom message should not resolve the same as a shallow compliment. Tests should verify that deltas stay within allowed ranges, flags are valid known IDs, and malformed responses fall back gracefully. Progression control model: - Gemini controls emotional interpretation and proposed stat movement. - The engine clamps deltas to allowed ranges. - The route graph controls which scenes exist. - Gemini can recommend route pressure, such as `toward_confession`, `toward_friendship`, `needs_repair`, or `opens_secret`. - The engine converts repeated Gemini recommendations into real flags or route movement. Remove from prior plan: - No custom safety-gate layer. - No extra moderation classifier. - No refusal logic beyond normal Gemini behavior. - Keep only operational validation: valid JSON, non-empty reply, known flag IDs, numeric bounds, timeout fallback. Quality gates for Gemini progression: - Same route node with different player text should produce meaningfully different deltas. - Gemini-suggested flags must be limited to the route’s known flag registry. - The system should log enough metadata to debug weird progression without storing API keys or raw secrets. - If Gemini fails, the game should preserve state and use a neutral fallback, not advance incorrectly.
A

Plan adjustment accepted. I’d update the previous plan in three ways: - Expand **1. Authored Route Structure** substantially. - Replace **15. AI Consistency And Safety Boundaries** with **15. Gemini-Guided Progression**. - Keep deterministic structure for route availability and save integrity, but let Gemini meaningfully influence emotional interpretation, stat deltas, flags, and route pressure. **Revised 1. Authored Route Structure** Requirements: The game should use detailed branching routes, not just broad route phases. Each Bagger needs a real visual-novel route graph with a main romance branch, a friendship branch, a crisis branch, multiple date/event branches, and at least one secret branch. The writing target should be “Japanese visual novel translated into English”: understated, symbolic, emotionally precise, sometimes awkward in a deliberate localized way, and built around recurring motifs rather than generic AI romance. Implementation: Define route graphs as structured authored data. Each scene node should include `id`, `route`, `chapter`, `premise`, `location`, `timeAffinity`, `requiredFlags`, `blockedByFlags`, `statHints`, `choices`, `nextCandidates`, `motifs`, and `translationStyleNotes`. Gemini writes the prose from this authored scene skeleton, but the breadth, route identity, conflicts, and consequences come from human-authored structure. Quality gates: Each Bagger should have at least 20 route nodes: 8 main romance scenes, 4 friendship scenes, 4 crisis/recovery scenes, 3 optional date scenes, and 1 secret scene. Every major branch should create at least two later consequences. Route maps should be reviewed manually before implementation to confirm that routes have breadth, replay value, and distinct emotional identities. Aurora route breadth: - Main route: slow trust, observatory memory, fear of being left behind, moonlit confession. - Friendship route: becoming her night-shift companion without romance pressure. - Crisis branch: the player rushes her emotional pace and she shuts down. - Date branches: puddle stargazing, old telescope repair, silent midnight excavation. - Secret branch: the “crater garden” scene unlocked by patience, low broken promises, and repeated quiet choices. Brummbert route breadth: - Main route: protector learns to accept protection. - Friendship route: reliable worksite partner and emotional anchor. - Crisis branch: old rescue trauma resurfaces after a storm event. - Date branches: tunnel walk, warm oil maintenance, rescue-lamp picnic. - Secret branch: the “tunnel song” scene unlocked by courage, rescue flags, and never mocking his softness. Mira route breadth: - Main route: poetic distance becomes chosen intimacy. - Friendship route: shared maps and quiet intellectual companionship. - Crisis branch: she retreats after feeling emotionally misread. - Date branches: river stone walk, clay-layer poem, underground stream search. - Secret branch: the “spring beneath the quarry” scene unlocked by depth, memory flags, and giving meaningful stones at the right time. Writing implementation: Add a route-level style bible. Aurora uses moonlight, glass, old maps, puddles, observatory dust. Brummbert uses tunnel echoes, rescue lamps, warm engine oil, brake lights, pressure gauges. Mira uses river stones, folded maps, clay, hidden water, sediment lines. Gemini prompts should not ask for “romantic emotional prose” generically; they should ask for “localized ADV visual-novel dialogue adapted from an authored Japanese route scene.” Writing quality gates: - Ban generic AI-ish lines like “our bond grows stronger” unless used mechanically outside dialogue. - Require concrete sensory details in every major scene. - Require recurring motifs to return with changed meaning later. - Require choices to be emotionally distinct, not “nice / nicer / nicest.” - Review sample outputs for the localized VN feel: short pauses, indirect confession, object symbolism, and restrained emotional escalation. **Revised 15. Gemini-Guided Progression** Requirements: Gemini should influence progression, not only generate flavor text. It should interpret player wording and selected choices to determine emotional impact, partial stat deltas, possible new flags, and route pressure. The deterministic engine still protects save integrity and known route structure, but Gemini should have real agency inside bounded systems. Implementation: Change `/api/interact` so Gemini returns structured output such as `reply`, `emotionalRead`, `bondDelta`, `trustDelta`, `warmthDelta`, `depthDelta`, `courageDelta`, `suggestedFlags`, `routePressure`, `nextTone`, `memory`, and `visual`. The engine clamps numerical deltas to route-defined bounds and only accepts known flag IDs, but the actual emotional reading and stat movement should come from Gemini. Quality gates: The same menu choice should produce different outcomes depending on the player’s custom wording and relationship context. A vulnerable confession, a shallow compliment, and a careless joke should not resolve identically. Tests should verify that Gemini can affect progression while staying within valid stat ranges and known route flags. Progression model: - Authored route graph defines possible scenes. - Calendar and route state define available scenes. - Gemini interprets emotional meaning inside the current scene. - Gemini proposes deltas and flags. - Engine clamps deltas, validates known flags, persists state, and selects the next candidate scene. - Repeated Gemini `routePressure` values can push toward romance, friendship, repair, crisis, or secret branch. Remove from the old plan: - No extra custom safety-gate layer. - No additional moderation classifier. - No heavy output policing beyond operational checks. - Keep only technical validation: valid JSON, known keys, known flags, numeric bounds, non-empty text, and timeout fallback. Quality gates for Gemini progression: - Gemini-driven deltas must be visible to the player through stat feedback. - Suggested flags must affect future route availability. - Route pressure must be accumulated and used, not ignored. - Failed Gemini responses should preserve the current scene or use a neutral fallback without corrupting progression. - Debug logs should help diagnose progression issues without exposing API keys.

gpt-5.5 | agent=plan | finish=stop
▶ Reasoning
**Structuring the Document**

I'm thinking about using headings and flat bullets, but no nested bullets, and I can use numbered sections along with bold labels. I don’t want any blanks in there either. 

I should mention I'm ready to build, but I won’t extend the core scope, except for the internal route map and test harness. 

Also, I need to clarify that we're not including audio, save slots, or CG gallery, mentioning that server persistence will differ from save slots. What if the user prefers no persistence beyond local?
I’m ready to build it. I would not expand the player-facing scope further before implementation; this is already a substantial dating-sim conversion. The only extra I’d add is internal: a route-map/debug view and automated playthrough fixtures, because this plan needs tooling to stay coherent. Below is the revised full plan, excluding audio, save slots, and CG gallery. ## Core Architecture The whole game should move to a deterministic-plus-Gemini hybrid. The authored game system owns route structure, calendars, available scenes, known flags, inventory, endings, and persistence integrity. Gemini owns expressive scene writing and a meaningful part of emotional interpretation: stat deltas, suggested flags, route pressure, scene tone, and memories. The central state should contain `player`, `day`, `period`, `currentRoute`, `lockedRoute`, `relationships`, `playerStats`, `flags`, `inventory`, `currency`, `backlog`, `eventsSeen`, `calendarState`, `routePressure`, and `endingState`. The frontend should render this state and request scene resolution from the backend. The backend should validate state changes, call Gemini, clamp Gemini outputs, and return a complete scene result. The game loop becomes: choose schedule/date/gift/dialogue, resolve calendar or route event, ask Gemini for emotional interpretation and scene prose, clamp and apply stat/flag changes, append backlog and memories, advance time, check lock-in and ending conditions. This gives the game authored VN structure while keeping the Baggers emotionally reactive. ## 1. Authored Branching Route Structure **Requirements:** Each Bagger needs a detailed route graph, not a simple affection meter. Aurora, Brummbert, and Mira should each have a main romance route, a complete friendship route, at least two emotional sub-branches, a crisis branch, recovery scenes, optional dates, and a rare secret branch. The player should feel that choices cause route drift, not just numeric increases. **Implementation:** Create route definitions as structured data. Each scene node should include `id`, `route`, `chapter`, `premise`, `location`, `periodAffinity`, `requiredFlags`, `blockedByFlags`, `statHints`, `motifs`, `choiceSet`, `nextCandidates`, `routePressureEffects`, and `translationStyleNotes`. Gemini writes the actual scene text from these authored premises, but the route graph defines breadth, structure, emotional continuity, and consequences. **Quality Gates:** Each Bagger should ship with at least 20 scene nodes: 8 main romance scenes, 4 friendship scenes, 4 crisis/recovery scenes, 3 optional date scenes, and 1 secret scene. Every major branch should create at least two later consequences. We should manually review route maps before implementation and run simulated playthroughs to prove every branch is reachable. Aurora route breadth should focus on patience, observatory memories, fear of abandonment, silence as intimacy, and a secret moonlit crater-garden scene. Her writing motifs are moonlight, glass, puddles, star maps, observatory dust, and the quiet sound of hydraulics cooling. Brummbert route breadth should focus on protection, old rescue trauma, learning to accept softness, post-storm vulnerability, and a secret tunnel-song scene. His motifs are tunnel echoes, rescue lamps, warm engine oil, brake lights, pressure gauges, and heavy machinery trying to be gentle. Mira route breadth should focus on poetic distance, river stones, clay layers, forgotten maps, emotional misreading, and a secret underground spring scene. Her motifs are folded maps, sediment lines, river stones, clay, hidden water, and the feeling that the earth remembers. The writing should feel like Japanese VN text translated into English: emotionally restrained, concrete, slightly indirect, and symbolic. Avoid generic AI phrasing like “our bond grows stronger” in dialogue. Prefer lines like: “The bucket stopped just above the mud. For a while, neither of us moved.” ## 2. Multiple Endings **Requirements:** Each Bagger needs multiple endings: bad end, missed-route end, friendship end, normal romance end, true romance end, and secret ending. Endings should depend on route completion, Gemini-influenced emotional stats, route pressure, kept promises, broken promises, date history, and final choices. **Implementation:** Add an `endingResolver` that runs at Day 30 or after a finale/confession event. It should inspect `lockedRoute`, `bond`, `trust`, `warmth`, `depth`, `courage`, required route flags, crisis recovery flags, secret flags, missed events, and accumulated Gemini route pressure. Gemini may write the ending scene, but the resolved ending type comes from the game engine. **Quality Gates:** Every ending must have documented conditions and at least one test fixture. A high-affection but emotionally shallow route should not automatically get the true ending. A player who builds friendship but avoids romance should get a satisfying friendship ending, not a failure screen. ## 3. Event Calendar **Requirements:** The game needs fixed calendar events that create anticipation and missed opportunities. Add maintenance day, rain day, night festival, inspection day, activation-day birthdays, storm rescue, route-specific night events, and final confession day. Some events should be known in advance; others can surprise the player. **Implementation:** Add a `calendarRegistry` keyed by `day` and `period`. Calendar events should take priority over generic route scenes. Some events should be global, while others should require route flags or relationship thresholds. The UI should show known upcoming events without revealing secret unlocks. **Quality Gates:** Fixed events must trigger exactly once unless marked repeatable. Missing important events should set flags like `missed_festival_aurora` or `missed_storm_rescue_brummbert`. Automated time-advance tests should confirm that calendar events correctly override normal scheduling. ## 4. Scheduling And Stat Training **Requirements:** The player should choose how to spend each time period. Options can include work shift, maintenance study, courage training, scrap collecting, rest, route visit, gift shopping, and date planning. This creates classical dating-sim time pressure. **Implementation:** Add protagonist stats: `mechanics`, `charm`, `patience`, `courage`, `focus`, and `fatigue`. Scheduling actions should deterministically affect these stats and sometimes produce currency or inventory items. Route scenes can require both relationship stats and protagonist stats. **Quality Gates:** The player should not be able to max all stats in one run. At least three important events per route should require protagonist stats. Simulated playthroughs should cover optimal, casual, and neglectful schedules to ensure routes are achievable but not automatic. ## 5. Affection And Stat Gates **Requirements:** Important route events should require meaningful thresholds. Aurora’s observatory scene might require `trust >= 35`, `patience >= 20`, and `aurora_quiet_promise`. Brummbert’s storm scene might require `courage >= 30`. Mira’s underground spring path might require `depth >= 55` and specific stone memories. **Implementation:** Implement reusable condition objects such as `{ stat: 'trust', gte: 35 }`, `{ playerStat: 'patience', gte: 20 }`, `{ flag: 'aurora_quiet_promise' }`, `{ dayGte: 8 }`, and `{ routePressure: 'toward_romance', gte: 3 }`. The event resolver should evaluate these before offering scenes. **Quality Gates:** Locked events should not be reachable early. Boundary tests should verify exact thresholds. The UI should sometimes provide soft hints like “Aurora seems to need more time” without exposing raw formulas. ## 6. Route Lock-In **Requirements:** Early route exploration should remain possible, but the game should eventually lock into one romance route. Other Baggers should remain available for friendship/support scenes, but their romance progression should stop. Lock-in should feel intentional and emotionally significant. **Implementation:** Add `lockedRoute` and per-Bagger `commitmentScore`. Repeated dates, confession-adjacent choices, high Gemini romance pressure, and route-specific promises increase commitment. Before irreversible lock-in, show a clear VN-style warning scene. **Quality Gates:** One casual interaction must not lock the player in. Finale scenes must only appear for the locked route. Manual QA should verify that post-lock scenes for non-route Baggers feel respectful rather than abruptly disabled. ## 7. Date-Location System **Requirements:** Dates should involve selecting a Bagger, a location, and optionally a gift. Each location should have tags and each Bagger should have preferences. Good matches should deepen the route; poor matches should produce awkward but interesting scenes. **Implementation:** Add locations like observatory, old tunnel, riverbed, workshop, night festival, scrapyard, hill road, rain shelter, quarry edge, and maintenance garage. Each has tags such as `quiet`, `public`, `technical`, `dangerous`, `romantic`, `memory`, `water`, `rescue`, or `stars`. Gemini receives location tags and relationship context. **Quality Gates:** Each Bagger needs preferred, neutral, and disliked locations. Date outcomes should visibly differ in prose, stats, flags, and future references. QA should run one good, neutral, and bad date per Bagger. ## 8. Gift Economy And Inventory **Requirements:** Add inventory, currency, gift shopping, rare finds, route-critical gifts, liked gifts, and disliked gifts. Gifts should not be a simple affection button; timing, route phase, location, and emotional context should matter. **Implementation:** Add item definitions with tags like `kiesel`, `tool`, `oil`, `star-map`, `rescue-badge`, `river-stone`, `glass-charm`, `lamp-bulb`, and `folded-map`. Scheduling actions can earn currency or discover items. Gift resolution should combine deterministic preference rules with Gemini’s emotional interpretation. **Quality Gates:** Gifts must be consumed correctly and persist across reloads. Each Bagger should have at least 5 liked gifts, 2 disliked gifts, and 1 route-critical gift. Tests should verify currency, inventory changes, gift reactions, and route unlocks. ## 9. Hidden Flags And Promise System **Requirements:** The game should track meaningful hidden state: promises made, promises kept, promises broken, missed dates, favorite locations, emotional boundaries, jealousy, accepted gifts, crisis recovery, and secret-route hints. These should matter later. **Implementation:** Add typed flags: `promise`, `keptPromise`, `brokenPromise`, `memory`, `mistake`, `route`, `secret`, `jealousy`, `boundary`, and `crisis`. Gemini can suggest flags from an allowed registry, while the engine validates and applies them. **Quality Gates:** Every major route event should set or clear at least one meaningful flag. Broken promises must be referenced later in dialogue or ending resolution. Suggested Gemini flags must be known IDs, never arbitrary strings. ## 10. Backlog And Dialogue History **Requirements:** Add a VN-style backlog showing prior narration, player choices, Bagger dialogue, day, period, location, route, and speaker. This is separate from memories: backlog is literal history, memories are emotional summaries. **Implementation:** Add `state.backlog` entries like `{ type, speaker, text, day, period, location, route, choiceId }`. Every visible scene line and player choice should append to it. Add a backlog panel with route filtering and a reasonable cap, such as 300 entries. **Quality Gates:** Backlog must persist across reloads and never include API keys, raw prompts, or backend debug details. It should remain performant on mobile. Manual QA should confirm every visible dialogue appears in the backlog. ## 11. Presentation Polish **Requirements:** The game should feel like a real VN/dating sim, not a form wrapped around an API. Add route title cards, fade transitions, dramatic pauses, choice reveal animations, affection-change feedback, scene transition text, and expressive 3D model poses. **Implementation:** Create a scene presentation layer with `showNarration`, `showDialogue`, `showChoices`, `transitionToLocation`, `showStatChange`, and `applyPose`. The 3D Bagger models should support poses such as listening, shy, proud, guarded, digging, crisis, and confession. **Quality Gates:** Animations must respect reduced-motion settings. The game must remain playable if WebGL fails. Mobile layout should preserve VN readability and not bury choices below excessive UI. ## 12. Failure Pressure And Missed Opportunities **Requirements:** The game needs stakes. Ignoring Baggers, missing promised dates, overworking, choosing incompatible actions, or reaching Day 30 without commitment should cause emotional consequences. Failure should be fair and foreshadowed. **Implementation:** Track `neglect`, `missedPromises`, `fatigue`, `routeDeadlineMisses`, and `unresolvedCrisis`. Calendar events can expire and set missed-event flags. Gemini can write disappointment or distance, while the engine controls penalties and ending eligibility. **Quality Gates:** A neglectful playthrough should reliably produce missed-route or friendship endings. One bad choice should not destroy a strong route. The UI should warn before major irreversible failures. ## 13. Protagonist Setup **Requirements:** At new game start, the player should choose name, address style, and personality inclination. Personality options can include earnest, shy, bold, practical, and poetic. These should affect starting stats and Gemini tone. **Implementation:** Add a start screen if no player profile exists. Store `player.name`, `player.address`, `player.style`, and starting stat bonuses. Include the player profile in Gemini prompts so Baggers can address the player consistently. **Quality Gates:** Names must be sanitized and length-limited. Empty names should fall back gracefully. Reload, reset, restore, and special-character cases should be tested. ## 14. Server-Side Persistence **Requirements:** Browser-local saves are fragile. Add server-side persistence without implementing save slots. Use one anonymous save token that lets the player restore progress on another device. This is not multiple manual save slots; it is durable continuation. **Implementation:** Add endpoints like `POST /api/save`, `GET /api/save/:token`, and `POST /api/restore`. Store JSON saves under `/opt/bagger-api/data/saves` or use SQLite with schema versioning. The frontend keeps the token locally and offers a restore-token UI. **Quality Gates:** Saves must be atomic, size-limited, schema-versioned, and recoverable after service restart. Invalid tokens and corrupted saves must fail safely. No API key, raw Gemini prompt, or sensitive server detail should ever be stored in saves. ## 15. Gemini-Guided Progression **Requirements:** Gemini should influence progression meaningfully. It should interpret player wording, selected choices, current route state, memories, and emotional context to propose stat deltas, suggested flags, route pressure, scene tone, and a memory. The game should not treat all “compliments” or “confessions” the same. **Implementation:** Change `/api/interact` so Gemini returns structured data: `reply`, `emotionalRead`, `bondDelta`, `trustDelta`, `warmthDelta`, `depthDelta`, `courageDelta`, `suggestedFlags`, `routePressure`, `nextTone`, `memory`, and `visual`. The engine clamps deltas to scene-specific ranges and accepts only known flags, but Gemini’s emotional interpretation should materially affect stats and route direction. **Quality Gates:** The same menu choice with different player text should produce different outcomes. A vulnerable message, shallow compliment, careless joke, and sincere promise should not resolve identically. Tests should verify delta bounds, known flag IDs, route-pressure accumulation, and graceful fallback if Gemini returns malformed JSON. ## Build Readiness I would build this in phases: 1. Foundation: state schema, route graph format, event resolver, Gemini structured response. 2. Core game systems: calendar, scheduling, stat gates, route lock, endings. 3. Relationship systems: gifts, dates, promises, hidden flags, Gemini progression. 4. UX systems: protagonist setup, backlog, presentation polish, server persistence. 5. Content pass: full route maps, writing style bible, route-specific motifs, ending prose. 6. QA pass: automated playthrough fixtures, route reachability checks, mobile/WebGL fallback, production service verification. I’m ready to build from this plan. I would not extend the public feature scope more before implementation; the next useful extension is internal tooling for route maps and playthrough simulation so the route graph does not become impossible to balance.
A
Assistant
gpt-5.5 | agent=plan | finish=stop
The revised plan above is the version I’d build from. As a game dev: I would **not extend the player-facing scope further right now**. It already covers the missing classical dating-sim pillars: branching routes, endings, calendar, scheduling, stat gates, route lock-in, dates, gifts, hidden flags, backlog, presentation, failure pressure, protagonist setup, persistence, and Gemini-guided progression. The one thing I would add before implementation is **internal tooling**, not another feature: - A route-map/debug view to inspect scene nodes, flags, gates, and next-scene candidates. - Automated playthrough simulations to verify that each ending and branch is reachable. - A small writing style bible and “bad phrase” checklist so Gemini output keeps the Japanese-VN-localization feel instead of drifting into generic AI romance. With that, I’m ready to build it.
