gpt-5.5 | agent=plan
U
User
Add the debug tooling (maybe a cli or api?) for yourself to the plan and make a the first step the style bible. Describe the style bible in detail in the plan. With these two extra things the plan will have 17 steps. Show me the full plan with all the details we have
A
Assistant
gpt-5.5 | agent=plan | finish=stop
Below is the full revised 17-step production plan. I’m ready to build from this, but this is still plan-only. **Build Philosophy** The game should become a deterministic-plus-Gemini dating sim. The authored engine owns structure: route graphs, calendar, available scenes, known flags, inventory, endings, persistence, and debug tooling. Gemini owns expressive scene writing and meaningful emotional interpretation: stat deltas, suggested flags, route pressure, memories, tone, and moment-to-moment nuance. The shared state model should include `schemaVersion`, `player`, `day`, `period`, `currentRoute`, `lockedRoute`, `relationships`, `playerStats`, `flags`, `inventory`, `currency`, `backlog`, `eventsSeen`, `calendarState`, `routePressure`, `endingState`, and `debugMeta`. The frontend renders state, the backend validates and resolves state transitions, and Gemini receives a curated scene context rather than raw save data. The main loop should be: choose schedule/date/gift/dialogue, resolve calendar or route event, call Gemini for scene prose plus emotional interpretation, clamp and validate Gemini output, apply stats/flags/route pressure, append backlog and memories, advance time, check route lock and endings, persist state, expose debug traces for development. ## 1. Style Bible **Requirements:** The style bible is the first deliverable because it defines how every route, scene premise, Gemini prompt, choice label, and ending should sound. The goal is not “AI romance”; the goal is “a sincere Japanese visual novel localized into English,” adapted to emotionally sentient excavators. The prose should be restrained, concrete, slightly indirect, emotionally specific, and built around recurring physical motifs. **Requirements:** The style should avoid generic therapy-chat, corporate wholesomeness, and obvious LLM phrasing. Characters should rarely state emotions abstractly. They should reveal emotion through action, hesitation, machinery behavior, weather, object symbolism, and short dialogue. A Bagger should not say “our bond is becoming stronger”; instead, the scene should show a bucket lowering, a lamp staying on, or a track stopping before crushing a small stone. **Requirements:** The style bible should define global tone rules, route-specific motifs, diction rules, dialogue rules, narration rules, choice-writing rules, forbidden phrases, emotional pacing, localization flavor, and examples of good and bad lines. It should also define how German UI can coexist with English-localized VN prose if we keep the site German-facing but the writing target English-like Japanese VN translation. If the final game remains German-language, the style bible should specify “German text that feels translated from Japanese VN prose,” with similar restraint and concrete imagery. **Implementation:** Create a `styleBible` data object or document consumed by prompts and by route authorship. It should include global constraints: short ADV-style paragraphs, no markdown, no speaker labels inside Gemini output, no exposition dumps, no “as an AI,” no direct stat references, and no meta-romance language. It should define scene rhythm: opening image, small reaction, emotional turn, lingering object or silence. **Implementation:** Route-specific style: Aurora uses moonlight, glass, puddles, old star maps, observatory dust, quiet hydraulics, lens reflections, and fear of abandonment. Her language should be careful, elliptical, and slow to trust. Brummbert uses tunnel echoes, warm engine oil, rescue lamps, pressure gauges, brake lights, and weight used gently. His language should be plain, protective, and embarrassed by tenderness. Mira uses river stones, sediment lines, folded maps, clay, underground springs, hidden water, and the earth remembering. Her language should be poetic but not purple, precise but distant until trust grows. **Implementation:** Add a bad-phrase list and replacement guidance. Bad examples: “I feel a deep connection,” “our bond grows stronger,” “you make my heart race,” “this means so much to me,” “I am here to support you.” Better examples: “The warning lamp blinked once, then stayed lit,” “My bucket stopped before it touched the mud,” “I kept the small stone in the dry part of my cab,” “The tunnel did not echo as loudly when you stood there.” **Quality Gates:** Before route implementation, create at least three sample scene outputs per Bagger: early route, crisis, and near-confession. Review them against the style bible. Reject samples that read like generic AI chat, over-explain feelings, mention stats, or ignore motifs. **Quality Gates:** Every authored scene node should include at least one motif hint and one tone note. Every major Gemini prompt should include a compressed style-bible excerpt. The debug tooling should be able to show which style notes were sent for a scene. **Quality Gates:** The final QA pass should include a writing review checklist: concrete sensory detail, route motif, emotional subtext, no generic AI phrase, no direct stat language, no unexplained route jump, and at least one line that feels memorable. ## 2. Authored Branching Route Structure **Requirements:** Each Bagger needs a detailed route graph, not a simple affection meter. Aurora, Brummbert, and Mira should each have a main romance route, a complete friendship route, at least two emotional sub-branches, a crisis branch, recovery scenes, optional dates, and a rare secret branch. The player should feel that choices cause route drift, not just numeric increases. **Requirements:** Branch breadth matters. The player should be able to develop different kinds of relationships with the same Bagger: patient slow-burn, bold pursuit, quiet friendship, repair-after-hurt, missed-chance melancholy, and secret intimacy. The romance route should not be the only satisfying path; friendship routes should feel complete and intentionally written. **Implementation:** Create route definitions as structured data. Each scene node should include `id`, `route`, `chapter`, `premise`, `location`, `periodAffinity`, `requiredFlags`, `blockedByFlags`, `statHints`, `motifs`, `choiceSet`, `nextCandidates`, `routePressureEffects`, and `translationStyleNotes`. Gemini writes the actual scene text from these authored premises, but the route graph defines breadth, structure, emotional continuity, and consequences. **Implementation:** Aurora route breadth should focus on patience, observatory memories, fear of abandonment, silence as intimacy, and a secret moonlit crater-garden scene. Brummbert route breadth should focus on protection, old rescue trauma, learning to accept softness, post-storm vulnerability, and a secret tunnel-song scene. Mira route breadth should focus on poetic distance, river stones, clay layers, forgotten maps, emotional misreading, and a secret underground spring scene. **Implementation:** Each route should have node categories: `intro`, `daily`, `date`, `threshold`, `crisis`, `repair`, `friendship`, `romance`, `finale`, and `secret`. The resolver should choose candidate nodes based on calendar, gates, route pressure, and flags. Gemini should never invent a new route node; it can only interpret and write within the selected authored node. **Quality Gates:** Each Bagger should ship with at least 20 scene nodes: 8 main romance scenes, 4 friendship scenes, 4 crisis/recovery scenes, 3 optional date scenes, and 1 secret scene. Every major branch should create at least two later consequences. We should manually review route maps before implementation and run simulated playthroughs to prove every branch is reachable. **Quality Gates:** No route should have a single obvious “best” sequence. At least three different successful playstyles should exist across the game: careful slow-burn, active dating, and friendship-first. Each route should have at least one meaningful choice where the emotionally kind option is not the same as the romance-maximizing option. **Quality Gates:** Writing review should confirm that route nodes do not sound interchangeable. If Aurora, Brummbert, and Mira can swap lines without anyone noticing, the node fails. ## 3. Multiple Endings **Requirements:** Each Bagger needs multiple endings: bad end, missed-route end, friendship end, normal romance end, true romance end, and secret ending. Endings should depend on route completion, Gemini-influenced emotional stats, route pressure, kept promises, broken promises, date history, crisis resolution, and final choices. **Requirements:** Endings should feel earned and coherent. A high affection score without emotional depth should not automatically produce the true ending. A friendship route should not feel like a failed romance; it should have its own emotional thesis. Bad endings should be melancholic and fair, not punitive shock outcomes. **Implementation:** Add an `endingResolver` that runs at Day 30 or after a finale/confession event. It should inspect `lockedRoute`, `bond`, `trust`, `warmth`, `depth`, `courage`, required route flags, crisis recovery flags, secret flags, missed events, and accumulated Gemini route pressure. Gemini may write the ending scene, but the resolved ending type comes from the game engine. **Implementation:** Ending scenes should be authored as high-level premises with style-bible notes and route motifs. Gemini writes final prose from a fixed ending type and route context. The engine should set `endingState` and stop normal time progression after an ending. **Quality Gates:** Every ending must have documented conditions and at least one test fixture. A simulated state for each ending should resolve consistently. The debug tooling should explain why a given ending was selected. **Quality Gates:** Every route should have at least one ending where the relationship remains meaningful without romance. Secret endings should require specific flags and not only high stats. **Quality Gates:** Ending prose should be manually reviewed for closure, motif payoff, and absence of generic “we did it” language. ## 4. Event Calendar **Requirements:** The game needs fixed calendar events that create anticipation and missed opportunities. Add maintenance day, rain day, night festival, inspection day, activation-day birthdays, storm rescue, route-specific night events, and final confession day. Some events should be known in advance; others can surprise the player. **Requirements:** Calendar events should create rhythm. Classical dating sims often build tension because the player knows time is limited. The player should sometimes choose between training, earning money, buying a gift, resting, or attending a route event. **Implementation:** Add a `calendarRegistry` keyed by `day` and `period`. Calendar events should take priority over generic route scenes. Some events should be global, while others should require route flags or relationship thresholds. The UI should show known upcoming events without revealing secret unlocks. **Implementation:** Missed calendar events should set flags like `missed_festival_aurora` or `missed_storm_rescue_brummbert`. Some missed events can be repaired later; others change the route permanently. Gemini should receive the event’s calendar significance as context. **Quality Gates:** Fixed events must trigger exactly once unless marked repeatable. Missing important events should have visible future consequences. Automated time-advance tests should confirm that calendar events correctly override normal scheduling. **Quality Gates:** The calendar UI should make near-term events legible without overwhelming the player. QA should verify that players can understand why an event happened and whether it was optional or important. **Quality Gates:** Event conflicts should be intentional and documented. If two important events can occur at the same time, the route implications must be clear. ## 5. Scheduling And Stat Training **Requirements:** The player should choose how to spend each time period. Options can include work shift, maintenance study, courage training, scrap collecting, rest, route visit, gift shopping, and date planning. This creates classical dating-sim time pressure. **Requirements:** Player stats should matter without becoming grind noise. Add `mechanics`, `charm`, `patience`, `courage`, `focus`, and `fatigue`. Different Baggers should value different traits, but no route should require perfect optimization. **Implementation:** Scheduling actions should deterministically affect stats and sometimes produce currency or inventory items. Work shift earns money but raises fatigue. Maintenance study raises mechanics and focus. Scrap collecting can find gifts. Rest reduces fatigue. Route visit triggers relationship scenes if available. **Implementation:** Fatigue should affect outcomes. High fatigue can reduce stat gains, worsen date results, or trigger soft failure warnings. Gemini can reference tiredness in scene prose, but deterministic code applies fatigue effects. **Quality Gates:** The player should not be able to max all stats in one run. At least three important events per route should require protagonist stats. Simulated playthroughs should cover optimal, casual, and neglectful schedules to ensure routes are achievable but not automatic. **Quality Gates:** Scheduling choices should have clear UI feedback: stat gained, fatigue changed, item found, event triggered. The player should understand tradeoffs. **Quality Gates:** Balance should avoid requiring a guide for normal romance endings. True/secret endings can require more precise play. ## 6. Affection And Stat Gates **Requirements:** Important route events should require meaningful thresholds. Aurora’s observatory scene might require `trust >= 35`, `patience >= 20`, and `aurora_quiet_promise`. Brummbert’s storm scene might require `courage >= 30`. Mira’s underground spring path might require `depth >= 55` and specific stone memories. **Requirements:** Gates should feel like emotional logic, not arbitrary locks. If a scene requires patience, the route should have shown that patience matters. If a scene requires courage, the story should create a reason courage is relevant. **Implementation:** Implement reusable condition objects such as `{ stat: 'trust', gte: 35 }`, `{ playerStat: 'patience', gte: 20 }`, `{ flag: 'aurora_quiet_promise' }`, `{ dayGte: 8 }`, and `{ routePressure: 'toward_romance', gte: 3 }`. The event resolver should evaluate these before offering scenes. **Implementation:** Locked events can be hidden, teased, or shown as unavailable depending on scene type. The UI should sometimes provide soft hints like “Aurora seems to need more time” without exposing raw formulas. **Quality Gates:** Locked events should not be reachable early. Boundary tests should verify exact thresholds. Route simulation should show that required stats can be reached naturally. **Quality Gates:** Gates should never deadlock the main game. If a player misses a romance route gate, friendship or repair content should still be available. **Quality Gates:** Debug tooling should explain why a scene is locked, including missing stats, flags, day constraints, and blocked flags. ## 7. Route Lock-In **Requirements:** Early route exploration should remain possible, but the game should eventually lock into one romance route. Other Baggers should remain available for friendship/support scenes, but their romance progression should stop. Lock-in should feel intentional and emotionally significant. **Requirements:** The game should warn before irreversible commitment. This should be framed as a VN emotional scene, not a mechanical popup only. The player should know they are choosing where their heart goes. **Implementation:** Add `lockedRoute` and per-Bagger `commitmentScore`. Repeated dates, confession-adjacent choices, high Gemini romance pressure, and route-specific promises increase commitment. Before irreversible lock-in, show a clear route-commitment scene. **Implementation:** Once locked, non-route Baggers can still appear in friendship, advice, or calendar scenes. Their romantic route nodes become blocked, but friendship nodes remain valid. This prevents the world from feeling like it vanishes outside the chosen romance. **Quality Gates:** One casual interaction must not lock the player in. Finale scenes must only appear for the locked route. Manual QA should verify that post-lock scenes for non-route Baggers feel respectful rather than abruptly disabled. **Quality Gates:** The debug view should show commitment score and lock-in reason. Tests should confirm lock-in thresholds and route blocking behavior. **Quality Gates:** Lock-in should be reversible only before the explicit commitment scene. After commitment, reversal should require a deliberate new playthrough. ## 8. Date-Location System **Requirements:** Dates should involve selecting a Bagger, a location, and optionally a gift. Each location should have tags and each Bagger should have preferences. Good matches should deepen the route; poor matches should produce awkward but interesting scenes. **Requirements:** Locations should feel authored and memorable, not just dropdown labels. Each should have visual identity, tags, calendar affinities, and possible route events. Some locations should unlock only after calendar or route progress. **Implementation:** Add locations like observatory, old tunnel, riverbed, workshop, night festival, scrapyard, hill road, rain shelter, quarry edge, and maintenance garage. Each has tags such as `quiet`, `public`, `technical`, `dangerous`, `romantic`, `memory`, `water`, `rescue`, or `stars`. **Implementation:** Bagger preferences map to tags and specific places. Aurora prefers quiet/stars/memory, Brummbert prefers rescue/technical/warmth, Mira prefers water/memory/quiet. Gemini receives location tags, preference fit, and route context. **Quality Gates:** Each Bagger needs preferred, neutral, and disliked locations. Date outcomes should visibly differ in prose, stats, flags, and future references. QA should run one good, neutral, and bad date per Bagger. **Quality Gates:** Poor dates should not always be pure punishment. They can reveal incompatibility, create repair opportunities, or open friendship branches. **Quality Gates:** Location tags and preference scoring should be testable without calling Gemini. ## 9. Gift Economy And Inventory **Requirements:** Add inventory, currency, gift shopping, rare finds, route-critical gifts, liked gifts, and disliked gifts. Gifts should not be a simple affection button; timing, route phase, location, and emotional context should matter. **Requirements:** Gifts should carry story meaning. Aurora’s star map, Brummbert’s rescue badge, and Mira’s river stone should become symbolic if given at the right moment. Wrong gifts should not be slapstick-only; they can reveal misunderstanding. **Implementation:** Add item definitions with tags like `kiesel`, `tool`, `oil`, `star-map`, `rescue-badge`, `river-stone`, `glass-charm`, `lamp-bulb`, and `folded-map`. Scheduling actions can earn currency or discover items. Shops can rotate stock by day. **Implementation:** Gift resolution should combine deterministic preference rules with Gemini’s emotional interpretation. The engine calculates preference fit and allowed stat ranges; Gemini interprets the emotional meaning and may suggest flags like `aurora_kept_star_map`. **Quality Gates:** Gifts must be consumed correctly and persist across reloads. Each Bagger should have at least 5 liked gifts, 2 disliked gifts, and 1 route-critical gift. Tests should verify currency, inventory changes, gift reactions, and route unlocks. **Quality Gates:** Route-critical gifts should have alternative recovery paths, unless they are only required for secret endings. Normal endings should not become impossible because of one missed shop item. **Quality Gates:** Debug tooling should show inventory, item tags, preference scoring, and flags created by gifts. ## 10. Hidden Flags And Promise System **Requirements:** The game should track meaningful hidden state: promises made, promises kept, promises broken, missed dates, favorite locations, emotional boundaries, jealousy, accepted gifts, crisis recovery, and secret-route hints. These should matter later. **Requirements:** Promises should be one of the emotional cores of the game. If the player promises to visit Aurora at night, protect Brummbert during a storm, or bring Mira a stone, the calendar and route system should remember. Keeping or breaking promises should affect dialogue, stats, and endings. **Implementation:** Add typed flags: `promise`, `keptPromise`, `brokenPromise`, `memory`, `mistake`, `route`, `secret`, `jealousy`, `boundary`, and `crisis`. Gemini can suggest flags from an allowed registry, while the engine validates and applies them. **Implementation:** Promise flags should include metadata: `route`, `createdDay`, `dueDay`, `duePeriod`, `kept`, `broken`, and `sceneId`. Time advancement should check due promises and mark missed ones. Gemini should be told about relevant kept/broken promises. **Quality Gates:** Every major route event should set or clear at least one meaningful flag. Broken promises must be referenced later in dialogue or ending resolution. Suggested Gemini flags must be known IDs, never arbitrary strings. **Quality Gates:** Promise due dates should be testable. If the player misses the due period, the flag should update exactly once. **Quality Gates:** The UI should surface active promises enough to be fair, without revealing hidden secret flags. ## 11. Backlog And Dialogue History **Requirements:** Add a VN-style backlog showing prior narration, player choices, Bagger dialogue, day, period, location, route, and speaker. This is separate from memories: backlog is literal history, memories are emotional summaries. **Requirements:** The backlog should support the VN reading experience. Players should be able to reread what a Bagger said, especially because Gemini output can be unique. The backlog should also help QA and route debugging. **Implementation:** Add `state.backlog` entries like `{ type, speaker, text, day, period, location, route, choiceId }`. Every visible scene line and player choice should append to it. Add a backlog panel with route filtering and a reasonable cap, such as 300 entries. **Implementation:** The backend can optionally return a `debugTraceId`, but the player backlog should not expose raw prompt data. Developer debug tooling can correlate trace IDs with sanitized scene resolution logs. **Quality Gates:** Backlog must persist across reloads and never include API keys, raw prompts, or backend debug details. It should remain performant on mobile. Manual QA should confirm every visible dialogue appears in the backlog. **Quality Gates:** Backlog filtering should work by route and day. Clearing backlog should only happen through new game/reset, not accidentally. **Quality Gates:** Backlog should not be treated as route memory by default. Memories are curated; backlog is transcript. ## 12. Presentation Polish **Requirements:** The game should feel like a real VN/dating sim, not a form wrapped around an API. Add route title cards, fade transitions, dramatic pauses, choice reveal animations, affection-change feedback, scene transition text, and expressive 3D model poses. **Requirements:** Presentation should support emotional pacing. A crisis scene should not transition like a normal date. A confession should use slower text, stronger framing, and a distinct pose. A missed promise should feel quiet and heavy. **Implementation:** Create a scene presentation layer with `showNarration`, `showDialogue`, `showChoices`, `transitionToLocation`, `showStatChange`, and `applyPose`. The 3D Bagger models should support poses such as listening, shy, proud, guarded, digging, crisis, and confession. **Implementation:** Add CSS transitions that respect `prefers-reduced-motion`. Add scene class names like `scene-crisis`, `scene-date`, `scene-confession`, and `scene-festival`. Gemini can suggest `visual`, but the presentation layer maps it to allowed effects. **Quality Gates:** Animations must respect reduced-motion settings. The game must remain playable if WebGL fails. Mobile layout should preserve VN readability and not bury choices below excessive UI. **Quality Gates:** Presentation effects should never block state updates or cause double submissions. QA should test slow network, Gemini timeout, and repeated taps. **Quality Gates:** Each route’s major scenes should have distinct presentation treatment. ## 13. Failure Pressure And Missed Opportunities **Requirements:** The game needs stakes. Ignoring Baggers, missing promised dates, overworking, choosing incompatible actions, or reaching Day 30 without commitment should cause emotional consequences. Failure should be fair and foreshadowed. **Requirements:** Failure should create drama, not just punishment. A crisis can become a repair branch. A missed date can lead to a quiet apology scene. Repeated neglect can push toward friendship or missed-route endings. **Implementation:** Track `neglect`, `missedPromises`, `fatigue`, `routeDeadlineMisses`, and `unresolvedCrisis`. Calendar events can expire and set missed-event flags. Gemini can write disappointment or distance, while the engine controls penalties and ending eligibility. **Implementation:** Add warning scenes before severe consequences. Example: if fatigue is too high, Brummbert notices the player is overworking. If Aurora has been ignored, she stops turning her cab light on. If Mira is misread repeatedly, she answers with maps instead of words. **Quality Gates:** A neglectful playthrough should reliably produce missed-route or friendship endings. One bad choice should not destroy a strong route. The UI should warn before major irreversible failures. **Quality Gates:** Failure states should be covered by simulation fixtures. Repair branches should be reachable after at least some mistakes. **Quality Gates:** Failure prose should avoid cruelty unless the player repeatedly chooses cruel actions. ## 14. Protagonist Setup **Requirements:** At new game start, the player should choose name, address style, and personality inclination. Personality options can include earnest, shy, bold, practical, and poetic. These should affect starting stats and Gemini tone. **Requirements:** The protagonist should feel present but flexible. The game should not overdefine them, but Baggers should be able to address them consistently and remember their style. **Implementation:** Add a start screen if no player profile exists. Store `player.name`, `player.address`, `player.style`, and starting stat bonuses. Include the player profile in Gemini prompts so Baggers can address the player consistently. **Implementation:** Personality styles can affect starting stats and choice flavor. Earnest gives warmth, shy gives patience, bold gives courage, practical gives mechanics, poetic gives depth/focus. The UI can still allow all choices regardless of style. **Quality Gates:** Names must be sanitized and length-limited. Empty names should fall back gracefully. Reload, reset, restore, and special-character cases should be tested. **Quality Gates:** Gemini should not overwrite profile data. Player name should not break JSON, HTML, or logs. **Quality Gates:** Protagonist setup should happen once per new game and be editable only through a deliberate profile/reset action. ## 15. Server-Side Persistence **Requirements:** Browser-local saves are fragile. Add server-side persistence without implementing save slots. Use one anonymous save token that lets the player restore progress on another device. This is not multiple manual save slots; it is durable continuation. **Requirements:** Persistence should protect player progress and support long routes. Saves should include route state, flags, inventory, backlog cap, player profile, and schema version. They should not include API keys, raw Gemini prompts, or server internals. **Implementation:** Add endpoints like `POST /api/save`, `GET /api/save/:token`, and `POST /api/restore`. Store JSON saves under `/opt/bagger-api/data/saves` or use SQLite with schema versioning. The frontend keeps the token locally and offers a restore-token UI. **Implementation:** Use atomic writes or SQLite transactions. Add schema validation and migration hooks. Add save-size limits and token entropy sufficient to prevent guessing. **Quality Gates:** Saves must be atomic, size-limited, schema-versioned, and recoverable after service restart. Invalid tokens and corrupted saves must fail safely. **Quality Gates:** A clean browser should be able to restore a token and continue the same game. Service restart should not lose saves. **Quality Gates:** Persistence tests should cover save, restore, invalid token, oversized save, schema migration, corrupted data, and concurrent save attempts. ## 16. Gemini-Guided Progression **Requirements:** Gemini should influence progression meaningfully. It should interpret player wording, selected choices, current route state, memories, and emotional context to propose stat deltas, suggested flags, route pressure, scene tone, and a memory. The game should not treat all compliments or confessions the same. **Requirements:** We do not need extra custom safety gates beyond Gemini’s built-in behavior. The game should still perform operational validation: valid JSON, known keys, known flags, numeric bounds, non-empty text, timeout fallback. This is not content moderation; it is save integrity. **Implementation:** Change `/api/interact` so Gemini returns structured data: `reply`, `emotionalRead`, `bondDelta`, `trustDelta`, `warmthDelta`, `depthDelta`, `courageDelta`, `suggestedFlags`, `routePressure`, `nextTone`, `memory`, and `visual`. The engine clamps deltas to scene-specific ranges and accepts only known flags, but Gemini’s emotional interpretation should materially affect stats and route direction. **Implementation:** Route pressure should be accumulated in categories like `toward_romance`, `toward_friendship`, `needs_repair`, `opens_secret`, `toward_crisis`, and `toward_lockin`. Repeated Gemini suggestions can unlock or prioritize branches, but only within the authored route graph. **Quality Gates:** The same menu choice with different player text should produce different outcomes. A vulnerable message, shallow compliment, careless joke, and sincere promise should not resolve identically. Tests should verify delta bounds, known flag IDs, route-pressure accumulation, and graceful fallback if Gemini returns malformed JSON. **Quality Gates:** Gemini-suggested flags must be from the route’s registry. Deltas must stay within scene-defined caps. Route pressure must be visible in debug tooling and must influence future scene selection. **Quality Gates:** If Gemini fails, the game should preserve current state or use a neutral fallback without corrupting progression or advancing incorrectly. ## 17. Debug Tooling And Route Simulation **Requirements:** This project needs developer-facing debug tooling because route graphs, flags, Gemini progression, calendar events, and endings will become too complex to balance manually. The tooling can be CLI, API, or both. It should be private/local by default and not exposed as a player feature. **Requirements:** The tools should answer practical questions: why is a scene locked, which endings are reachable, which flags were set, what did Gemini propose, which deltas were clamped, what route pressure changed, and what event will trigger next. It should help tune balance without editing save files by hand. **Implementation:** Add a CLI tool such as `/opt/bagger-api/tools/simulate.py` with commands: `list-routes`, `show-route aurora`, `validate-routes`, `simulate --route aurora --strategy romance`, `simulate --route mira --strategy neglect`, `ending-fixture true_aurora`, and `explain-scene --save token --scene aurora_observatory_2`. The CLI can load route definitions, evaluate gates, run deterministic simulations, and optionally use mock Gemini outputs for repeatability. **Implementation:** Add private API endpoints bound to localhost or protected by an environment flag: `GET /api/debug/state/:token`, `POST /api/debug/explain-scene`, `GET /api/debug/routes`, `POST /api/debug/simulate`, and `POST /api/debug/resolve-ending`. These should be disabled publicly unless `BAGGER_DEBUG=1` and ideally only available on `127.0.0.1`. **Implementation:** Add debug traces for each scene resolution: selected scene, candidate scenes, rejected scenes with reasons, Gemini emotional read, proposed deltas, clamped deltas, accepted flags, rejected flags, route pressure changes, calendar event consumed, time advanced, and ending checks. Store only sanitized traces with a cap. **Quality Gates:** `validate-routes` should fail if scenes reference unknown flags, impossible gates, missing next nodes, missing style notes, missing motifs, unknown locations, or unreachable endings. It should also detect dead ends unless marked as endings. **Quality Gates:** Simulations should cover at least strategies `romance`, `friendship`, `balanced`, `neglect`, `gift-heavy`, and `training-heavy`. Each Bagger should have at least one simulated path to normal romance, true romance, friendship, and bad/missed ending. **Quality Gates:** Debug endpoints must not expose Gemini API keys, raw prompts containing sensitive data, or unrestricted file access. Public Caddy routes should not expose debug APIs. CLI output can be more detailed because it is server-local. ## Build Phases Phase 1 should be the style bible, state schema, route-definition format, and debug validation scaffolding. This makes the rest of the work coherent before feature complexity grows. Phase 2 should add route graph resolution, calendar events, scheduling, stat gates, and route lock-in. At this point, the game should be playable with deterministic placeholder scene text. Phase 3 should integrate Gemini-guided progression with structured JSON output, route pressure, suggested flags, and style-bible prompt compression. This gives scenes emotional variability without losing structure. Phase 4 should add dates, gifts, inventory, promises, failure pressure, and endings. This is where the game becomes a real dating sim rather than a VN chat shell. Phase 5 should add protagonist setup, backlog, presentation polish, server-side persistence, and WebGL/presentation fallback refinement. Phase 6 should be content and QA: fill all route nodes, review writing samples, run route simulations, verify every ending, test mobile, test service restart, and verify production Caddy/API behavior.
A
Assistant
gpt-5.5 | agent=plan | finish=stop
Below is the full revised **17-step production plan**, with the requested additions: - **Step 1 is now the Style Bible** - **Step 17 is Debug Tooling / CLI / API** - **Gemini controls progression meaningfully** - **No extra custom safety gate layer** - **Authored routes are expanded into detailed branching route graphs** - **Audio, save slots, and CG gallery remain excluded** I’m ready to build from this plan. ## Core Architecture The game should become a deterministic-plus-Gemini dating sim. The authored engine owns route structure, calendars, available scenes, known flags, inventory, endings, persistence integrity, and debug tooling. Gemini owns expressive scene writing and meaningful emotional interpretation: stat deltas, suggested flags, route pressure, memories, tone, and moment-to-moment nuance. The shared state model should include `schemaVersion`, `player`, `day`, `period`, `currentRoute`, `lockedRoute`, `relationships`, `playerStats`, `flags`, `inventory`, `currency`, `backlog`, `eventsSeen`, `calendarState`, `routePressure`, `endingState`, and `debugMeta`. The main loop should be: choose schedule/date/gift/dialogue, resolve calendar or route event, call Gemini for scene prose plus emotional interpretation, clamp and validate Gemini output structurally, apply stats/flags/route pressure, append backlog and memories, advance time, check route lock and endings, persist state, expose debug traces for development. ## 1. Style Bible **Requirements:** The style bible is the first deliverable because it defines how every route, scene premise, Gemini prompt, choice label, and ending should sound. The target is not generic “AI romance”; it is “a sincere Japanese visual novel localized into English,” adapted to emotionally sentient excavators. The prose should be restrained, concrete, slightly indirect, emotionally specific, and built around recurring physical motifs. **Requirements:** The writing should avoid therapy-chat, corporate wholesomeness, and obvious LLM phrasing. Characters should rarely state emotions abstractly. Emotion should appear through action, hesitation, machinery behavior, weather, object symbolism, and short dialogue. A Bagger should not say “our bond is becoming stronger”; the scene should show a bucket lowering, a lamp staying on, or a track stopping before crushing a small stone. **Requirements:** The style bible should define global tone rules, route-specific motifs, diction rules, dialogue rules, narration rules, choice-writing rules, forbidden phrases, emotional pacing, localization flavor, and examples of good and bad lines. If the UI remains German-facing, the bible should specify German prose that feels like localized Japanese VN writing: restrained, symbolic, concrete, and emotionally indirect. **Implementation:** Create a `styleBible` object or document consumed by prompts and route definitions. It should include global constraints: short ADV-style paragraphs, no markdown, no speaker labels inside Gemini output, no exposition dumps, no direct stat references, no meta-romance language, no “as an AI,” and no generic emotional summary lines. **Implementation:** Define scene rhythm: opening image, small mechanical reaction, emotional turn, lingering object or silence. Example rhythm: “The rain ticks on the cab roof. Aurora lowers her bucket, not quite touching the puddle. ‘If you come tomorrow too,’ she says, ‘I might believe tonight was real.’” **Implementation:** Route-specific style: Aurora uses moonlight, glass, puddles, old star maps, observatory dust, quiet hydraulics, lens reflections, and fear of abandonment. Her language is careful, elliptical, and slow to trust. Brummbert uses tunnel echoes, warm engine oil, rescue lamps, pressure gauges, brake lights, and weight used gently. His language is plain, protective, and embarrassed by tenderness. Mira uses river stones, sediment lines, folded maps, clay, underground springs, hidden water, and the earth remembering. Her language is poetic but not purple, precise but distant until trust grows. **Implementation:** Maintain a bad-phrase list and replacement guidance. Bad examples: “I feel a deep connection,” “our bond grows stronger,” “you make my heart race,” “this means so much to me,” “I am here to support you.” Better examples: “The warning lamp blinked once, then stayed lit,” “My bucket stopped before it touched the mud,” “I kept the small stone in the dry part of my cab,” “The tunnel did not echo as loudly when you stood there.” **Quality Gates:** Before route implementation, create at least three sample scene outputs per Bagger: early route, crisis, and near-confession. Reject samples that read like generic AI chat, over-explain feelings, mention stats, or ignore motifs. **Quality Gates:** Every authored scene node should include at least one motif hint and one tone note. Every major Gemini prompt should include a compressed style-bible excerpt. Debug tooling should show which style notes were sent for a scene. **Quality Gates:** Final QA should include a writing checklist: concrete sensory detail, route motif, emotional subtext, no generic AI phrase, no direct stat language, no unexplained route jump, and at least one memorable line. ## 2. Authored Branching Route Structure **Requirements:** Each Bagger needs a detailed route graph, not a simple affection meter. Aurora, Brummbert, and Mira should each have a main romance route, a complete friendship route, at least two emotional sub-branches, a crisis branch, recovery scenes, optional dates, and a rare secret branch. **Requirements:** Branch breadth matters. The player should be able to develop different kinds of relationships with the same Bagger: patient slow-burn, bold pursuit, quiet friendship, repair-after-hurt, missed-chance melancholy, and secret intimacy. **Implementation:** Create route definitions as structured data. Each scene node should include `id`, `route`, `chapter`, `premise`, `location`, `periodAffinity`, `requiredFlags`, `blockedByFlags`, `statHints`, `motifs`, `choiceSet`, `nextCandidates`, `routePressureEffects`, and `translationStyleNotes`. **Implementation:** Aurora route breadth should focus on patience, observatory memories, fear of abandonment, silence as intimacy, and a secret moonlit crater-garden scene. Brummbert route breadth should focus on protection, old rescue trauma, learning to accept softness, post-storm vulnerability, and a secret tunnel-song scene. Mira route breadth should focus on poetic distance, river stones, clay layers, forgotten maps, emotional misreading, and a secret underground spring scene. **Implementation:** Each route should have node categories: `intro`, `daily`, `date`, `threshold`, `crisis`, `repair`, `friendship`, `romance`, `finale`, and `secret`. The resolver should choose candidate nodes based on calendar, gates, route pressure, and flags. **Quality Gates:** Each Bagger should ship with at least 20 scene nodes: 8 main romance scenes, 4 friendship scenes, 4 crisis/recovery scenes, 3 optional date scenes, and 1 secret scene. **Quality Gates:** No route should have a single obvious best sequence. At least three successful playstyles should exist: careful slow-burn, active dating, and friendship-first. **Quality Gates:** If Aurora, Brummbert, and Mira can swap lines without anyone noticing, the node fails. ## 3. Multiple Endings **Requirements:** Each Bagger needs bad end, missed-route end, friendship end, normal romance end, true romance end, and secret ending. **Requirements:** Endings should depend on route completion, Gemini-influenced emotional stats, route pressure, kept promises, broken promises, date history, crisis resolution, and final choices. **Implementation:** Add an `endingResolver` that runs at Day 30 or after a finale/confession event. It should inspect `lockedRoute`, `bond`, `trust`, `warmth`, `depth`, `courage`, required route flags, crisis recovery flags, secret flags, missed events, and accumulated Gemini route pressure. **Implementation:** Gemini writes the ending prose from a resolved ending type, but cannot directly pick the ending. The engine sets `endingState` and stops normal time progression. **Quality Gates:** Every ending must have documented conditions and at least one test fixture. A high-affection but emotionally shallow route should not automatically get the true ending. **Quality Gates:** Secret endings should require specific route behavior, not just high stats. **Quality Gates:** Ending prose should be reviewed for motif payoff and closure. ## 4. Event Calendar **Requirements:** Add fixed calendar events: maintenance day, rain day, night festival, inspection day, activation-day birthdays, storm rescue, route-specific night events, and final confession day. **Requirements:** Calendar events should create anticipation and missed opportunities. Some should be known in advance; others can surprise the player. **Implementation:** Add a `calendarRegistry` keyed by `day` and `period`. Calendar events should take priority over generic route scenes. Some events should be global, while others require route flags or relationship thresholds. **Implementation:** Missed calendar events should set flags like `missed_festival_aurora` or `missed_storm_rescue_brummbert`. **Quality Gates:** Fixed events must trigger exactly once unless marked repeatable. **Quality Gates:** Missing important events should have visible later consequences. **Quality Gates:** Calendar conflict behavior must be documented and testable. ## 5. Scheduling And Stat Training **Requirements:** The player should choose how to spend each time period: work shift, maintenance study, courage training, scrap collecting, rest, route visit, gift shopping, or date planning. **Requirements:** Add player stats: `mechanics`, `charm`, `patience`, `courage`, `focus`, and `fatigue`. **Implementation:** Scheduling actions deterministically affect stats, fatigue, currency, and possible item finds. Work earns money but increases fatigue. Study raises mechanics/focus. Scrap collecting can find gifts. Rest lowers fatigue. **Implementation:** Fatigue should affect scene outcomes. Gemini can reference tiredness, while deterministic code applies fatigue effects. **Quality Gates:** The player should not be able to max all stats in one playthrough. **Quality Gates:** At least three important events per route should require protagonist stats. **Quality Gates:** Normal endings should be achievable without guide-level optimization. ## 6. Affection And Stat Gates **Requirements:** Important route events should require meaningful thresholds. Example: Aurora’s observatory scene needs `trust >= 35`, `patience >= 20`, and `aurora_quiet_promise`. **Requirements:** Gates should feel emotionally logical, not arbitrary. **Implementation:** Implement reusable condition objects such as `{ stat: 'trust', gte: 35 }`, `{ playerStat: 'patience', gte: 20 }`, `{ flag: 'aurora_quiet_promise' }`, `{ dayGte: 8 }`, and `{ routePressure: 'toward_romance', gte: 3 }`. **Implementation:** Locked events can be hidden, teased, or shown as unavailable depending on scene type. **Quality Gates:** Boundary tests should verify exact thresholds. **Quality Gates:** Gates should never deadlock the game; friendship or repair content should remain available. **Quality Gates:** Debug tooling should explain why a scene is locked. ## 7. Route Lock-In **Requirements:** Early exploration should be possible, but the game should eventually lock into one romance route. Other Baggers remain available for friendship/support scenes. **Requirements:** Lock-in must feel intentional and emotionally significant. **Implementation:** Add `lockedRoute` and per-Bagger `commitmentScore`. Repeated dates, confession-adjacent choices, high Gemini romance pressure, and route promises increase commitment. **Implementation:** Before irreversible lock-in, show a clear route-commitment scene. **Quality Gates:** One casual interaction must not lock the player in. **Quality Gates:** Finale scenes must only appear for the locked route. **Quality Gates:** Post-lock non-route Bagger scenes should still feel respectful and complete. ## 8. Date-Location System **Requirements:** Dates should involve selecting a Bagger, a location, and optionally a gift. Each location should have tags and Bagger preferences. **Requirements:** Locations should be authored and memorable, not just dropdown labels. **Implementation:** Add locations like observatory, old tunnel, riverbed, workshop, night festival, scrapyard, hill road, rain shelter, quarry edge, and maintenance garage. **Implementation:** Location tags include `quiet`, `public`, `technical`, `dangerous`, `romantic`, `memory`, `water`, `rescue`, and `stars`. Bagger preferences map to these tags. **Quality Gates:** Each Bagger needs preferred, neutral, and disliked locations. **Quality Gates:** Good, neutral, and poor dates should produce visibly different outcomes. **Quality Gates:** Poor dates should create awkward or repair content, not just punishment. ## 9. Gift Economy And Inventory **Requirements:** Add inventory, currency, gift shopping, rare finds, route-critical gifts, liked gifts, and disliked gifts. **Requirements:** Gifts should carry story meaning and should matter by timing and route context. **Implementation:** Add items like `kiesel`, `tool`, `oil`, `star-map`, `rescue-badge`, `river-stone`, `glass-charm`, `lamp-bulb`, and `folded-map`. **Implementation:** Gift resolution combines deterministic preference rules with Gemini’s emotional interpretation. **Quality Gates:** Gifts must be consumed correctly and persist across reloads. **Quality Gates:** Each Bagger should have at least 5 liked gifts, 2 disliked gifts, and 1 route-critical gift. **Quality Gates:** Normal endings should not become impossible because of one missed shop item. ## 10. Hidden Flags And Promise System **Requirements:** Track promises made, promises kept, promises broken, missed dates, favorite locations, emotional boundaries, jealousy, accepted gifts, crisis recovery, and secret-route hints. **Requirements:** Promises should be an emotional core of the game. **Implementation:** Add typed flags: `promise`, `keptPromise`, `brokenPromise`, `memory`, `mistake`, `route`, `secret`, `jealousy`, `boundary`, and `crisis`. **Implementation:** Promise flags should include `route`, `createdDay`, `dueDay`, `duePeriod`, `kept`, `broken`, and `sceneId`. **Quality Gates:** Every major route event should set or clear at least one meaningful flag. **Quality Gates:** Broken promises must affect later dialogue or ending resolution. **Quality Gates:** Gemini-suggested flags must be known IDs. ## 11. Backlog And Dialogue History **Requirements:** Add a VN-style backlog showing narration, player choices, Bagger dialogue, day, period, location, route, and speaker. **Requirements:** Backlog is literal transcript. Memories are summarized emotional facts. They must remain separate. **Implementation:** Add `state.backlog` entries like `{ type, speaker, text, day, period, location, route, choiceId }`. **Implementation:** Add a backlog panel with route filtering and a cap around 300 entries. **Quality Gates:** Backlog must persist across reloads. **Quality Gates:** Backlog must not include API keys, raw prompts, or backend debug details. **Quality Gates:** Every visible dialogue should appear in backlog. ## 12. Presentation Polish **Requirements:** Add route title cards, fades, dramatic pauses, choice reveal animations, affection-change feedback, scene transition text, and expressive 3D Bagger poses. **Requirements:** Presentation should support emotional pacing. **Implementation:** Create a scene presentation layer with `showNarration`, `showDialogue`, `showChoices`, `transitionToLocation`, `showStatChange`, and `applyPose`. **Implementation:** 3D model poses should include listening, shy, proud, guarded, digging, crisis, and confession. **Quality Gates:** Animations must respect reduced-motion settings. **Quality Gates:** WebGL failure should still leave the game playable. **Quality Gates:** Effects must not cause double submissions or broken state updates. ## 13. Failure Pressure And Missed Opportunities **Requirements:** Ignoring Baggers, missing promised dates, overworking, incompatible actions, or reaching Day 30 without commitment should have consequences. **Requirements:** Failure should create drama, not random punishment. **Implementation:** Track `neglect`, `missedPromises`, `fatigue`, `routeDeadlineMisses`, and `unresolvedCrisis`. **Implementation:** Add warning scenes before severe consequences. **Quality Gates:** A neglectful playthrough should reliably produce missed-route or friendship endings. **Quality Gates:** One bad choice should not destroy a strong route. **Quality Gates:** Repair branches should exist after some mistakes. ## 14. Protagonist Setup **Requirements:** At new game start, the player chooses name, address style, and personality inclination: earnest, shy, bold, practical, or poetic. **Requirements:** The protagonist should feel present but flexible. **Implementation:** Add a start screen if no profile exists. Store `player.name`, `player.address`, `player.style`, and starting stat bonuses. **Implementation:** Personality affects starting stats and Gemini tone, but does not hard-lock choices. **Quality Gates:** Names must be sanitized and length-limited. **Quality Gates:** Gemini must not overwrite profile data. **Quality Gates:** Profile setup should happen once per new game unless deliberately reset. ## 15. Server-Side Persistence **Requirements:** Add durable server-side persistence without adding save slots. Use one anonymous save token that can restore progress on another device. **Requirements:** Saves include route state, flags, inventory, capped backlog, player profile, and schema version. **Implementation:** Add endpoints like `POST /api/save`, `GET /api/save/:token`, and `POST /api/restore`. **Implementation:** Store JSON saves under `/opt/bagger-api/data/saves` or use SQLite with schema versioning and atomic writes. **Quality Gates:** Saves must be atomic, size-limited, schema-versioned, and recoverable after service restart. **Quality Gates:** Invalid tokens and corrupted saves must fail safely. **Quality Gates:** No API key, raw Gemini prompt, or sensitive server detail should be stored. ## 16. Gemini-Guided Progression **Requirements:** Gemini should influence progression meaningfully. It should interpret player wording, selected choices, route state, memories, and context to propose stat deltas, flags, route pressure, scene tone, and memory. **Requirements:** We do not add custom safety gates beyond Gemini’s built-in behavior. We only perform operational validation for save integrity. **Implementation:** Change `/api/interact` so Gemini returns structured data: `reply`, `emotionalRead`, `bondDelta`, `trustDelta`, `warmthDelta`, `depthDelta`, `courageDelta`, `suggestedFlags`, `routePressure`, `nextTone`, `memory`, and `visual`. **Implementation:** The engine clamps deltas to scene-specific ranges and accepts only known flags, but Gemini’s interpretation materially affects stats and route direction. **Implementation:** Route pressure categories include `toward_romance`, `toward_friendship`, `needs_repair`, `opens_secret`, `toward_crisis`, and `toward_lockin`. **Quality Gates:** The same menu choice with different text should produce different outcomes. **Quality Gates:** Gemini-suggested flags must be from the known route registry. **Quality Gates:** If Gemini fails, preserve state or use a neutral fallback without corrupting progression. ## 17. Debug Tooling And Route Simulation **Requirements:** This project needs developer-facing debug tooling because route graphs, flags, Gemini progression, calendar events, and endings will become complex. The tooling can be CLI, API, or both. It should be private/local by default. **Requirements:** The tooling should answer practical questions: why is a scene locked, which endings are reachable, which flags were set, what Gemini proposed, which deltas were clamped, what route pressure changed, and what event triggers next. **Implementation:** Add a CLI tool such as `/opt/bagger-api/tools/simulate.py` with commands: `list-routes` `show-route aurora` `validate-routes` `simulate --route aurora --strategy romance` `simulate --route mira --strategy neglect` `ending-fixture true_aurora` `explain-scene --save token --scene aurora_observatory_2` **Implementation:** Add private API endpoints bound to localhost or protected by `BAGGER_DEBUG=1`: `GET /api/debug/state/:token` `POST /api/debug/explain-scene` `GET /api/debug/routes` `POST /api/debug/simulate` `POST /api/debug/resolve-ending` **Implementation:** Add debug traces for each scene resolution: selected scene, candidate scenes, rejected scenes with reasons, Gemini emotional read, proposed deltas, clamped deltas, accepted flags, rejected flags, route pressure changes, calendar event consumed, time advanced, and ending checks. **Quality Gates:** `validate-routes` should fail if scenes reference unknown flags, impossible gates, missing next nodes, missing style notes, missing motifs, unknown locations, or unreachable endings. **Quality Gates:** Simulations should cover `romance`, `friendship`, `balanced`, `neglect`, `gift-heavy`, and `training-heavy`. **Quality Gates:** Debug endpoints must not expose Gemini API keys, raw prompts with sensitive data, or unrestricted file access. Public Caddy routes should not expose debug APIs. ## Build Phases Phase 1: style bible, state schema, route-definition format, and debug validation scaffolding. Phase 2: route graph resolver, calendar events, scheduling, stat gates, and route lock-in. Phase 3: Gemini-guided structured progression with stat deltas, flags, route pressure, and style-bible prompt compression. Phase 4: dates, gifts, inventory, promises, failure pressure, and endings. Phase 5: protagonist setup, backlog, presentation polish, server-side persistence, and WebGL fallback refinement. Phase 6: full content pass, route writing, simulation fixtures, ending verification, mobile testing, service restart testing, and production verification.
U
User
Store your full last message in two places: plan.md and a plan via your plan tool.
A

Authored Branching Route Structure\n+\n+- [ ] **Requirements:** Each Bagger needs a detailed route graph, not a simple affection meter. Aurora, Brummbert, and Mira should each have a main romance route, a complete friendship route, at least two emotional sub-branches, a crisis branch, recovery scenes, optional dates, and a rare secret branch.\n+\n+- [ ] **Requirements:** Branch breadth matters. Each scene node should include `id`, `route`, `chapter`, `premise`, `location`, `periodAffinity`, `requiredFlags`, `blockedByFlags`, `statHints`, `motifs`, `choiceSet`, `nextCandidates`, `routePressureEffects`, and `translationStyleNotes`.\n+\n+- [ ] **Implementation:** Aurora route breadth should focus on patience, observatory memories, fear of abandonment, silence as intimacy, and a secret moonlit crater-garden scene. The resolver should choose candidate nodes based on calendar, gates, route pressure, and flags.\n+\n+- [ ] **Quality Gates:** Each Bagger should ship with at least 20 scene nodes: 8 main romance scenes, 4 friendship scenes, 4 crisis/recovery scenes, 3 optional date scenes, and 1 secret scene.\n+\n+- [ ] **Quality Gates:** No route should have a single obvious best sequence. At least three successful playstyles should exist: careful slow-burn, active dating, and friendship-first.\n+\n+- [ ] **Quality Gates:** If Aurora, Brummbert, and Mira can swap lines without anyone noticing, the node fails.\n+\n+## 3. Multiple Endings\n+\n+- [ ] **Requirements:** Each Bagger needs bad end, missed-route end, friendship end, normal romance end, true romance end, and secret ending.\n+\n+- [ ] **Requirements:** Endings should depend on route completion, Gemini-influenced emotional stats, route pressure, kept promises, broken promises, date history, crisis resolution, and final choices.\n+\n+- [ ] **Implementation:** Add an `endingResolver` that runs at Day 30 or after a finale/confession event. It should inspect `lockedRoute`, `bond`, `trust`, `warmth`, `depth`, `courage`, required route flags, crisis recovery flags, secret flags, missed events, and accumulated Gemini route pressure.\n+\n+- [ ] **Implementation:** Gemini writes the ending prose from a resolved ending type, but cannot directly pick the ending. The engine sets `endingState` and stops normal time progression.\n+\n+- [ ] **Quality Gates:** Every ending must have documented conditions and at least one test fixture. A high-affection but emotionally shallow route should not automatically get the true ending.\n+\n+- [ ] **Quality Gates:** Secret endings should require specific route behavior, not just high stats.\n+\n+- [ ] **Quality Gates:** Ending prose should be reviewed for motif payoff and closure.\n+\n+## 4. Event Calendar\n+\n+- [ ] **Requirements:** Add fixed calendar events: maintenance day, rain day, night festival, inspection day, activation-day birthdays, storm rescue, route-specific night events, and final confession day.\n+\n+- [ ] **Requirements:** Calendar events should create anticipation and missed opportunities. Some should be known in advance; others can surprise the player.\n+\n+- [ ] **Implementation:** Add a `calendarRegistry` keyed by `day` and `period`. Calendar events should take priority over generic route scenes. Some events should be global, while others require route flags or relationship thresholds.\n+\n+- [ ] **Implementation:** Missed calendar events should set flags like `missed_festival_aurora` or `missed_storm_rescue_brummbert`.\n+\n+- [ ] **Quality Gates:** Fixed events must trigger exactly once unless marked repeatable.\n+\n+- [ ] **Quality Gates:** Missing important events should have visible later consequences.\n+\n+- [ ] **Quality Gates:** Calendar conflict behavior must be documented and testable.\n+\n+## 5. Scheduling And Stat Training\n+\n+- [ ] **Requirements:** The player should choose how to spend each time period: work shift, maintenance study, courage training, scrap collecting, rest, route visit, gift shopping, or date planning.\n+\n+- [ ] **Requirements:** Add player stats: `mechanics`, `charm`, `patience`, `courage`, `focus`, and `fatigue`.\n+\n+- [ ] **Implementation:** Scheduling actions deterministically affect stats, fatigue, currency, and possible item finds. Work earns money but increases fatigue. Study raises mechanics/focus. Scrap collecting can find gifts. Rest lowers fatigue.\n+\n+- [ ] **Implementation:** Fatigue should affect scene outcomes. Gemini can reference tiredness, while deterministic code applies fatigue effects.\n+\n+- [ ] **Quality Gates:** The player should not be able to max all stats in one playthrough.\n+\n+- [ ] **Quality Gates:** At least three important events per route should require protagonist stats.\n+\n+- [ ] **Quality Gates:** Normal endings should be achievable without guide-level optimization.\n+\n+## 6. Affection And Stat Gates\n+\n+- [ ] **Requirements:** Important route events should require meaningful thresholds. Example: Aurora's observatory scene needs `trust >= 35`, `patience >= 20`, and `aurora_quiet_promise`.\n+\n+- [ ] **Requirements:** Gates should feel emotionally logical, not arbitrary.\n+\n+- [ ] **Implementation:** Implement reusable condition objects such as `{ stat: 'trust', gte: 35 }`, `{ playerStat: 'patience', gte: 20 }`, `{ flag: 'aurora_quiet_promise' }`, `{ dayGte: 8 }`, and `{ routePressure: 'toward_romance', gte: 3 }`.\n+\n+- [ ] **Implementation:** Locked events can be hidden, teased, or shown as unavailable depending on scene type.\n+\n+- [ ] **Quality Gates:** Boundary tests should verify exact thresholds.\n+\n+- [ ] **Quality Gates:** Gates should never deadlock the game; friendship or repair content should remain available.\n+\n+- [ ] **Quality Gates:** Debug tooling should explain why a scene is locked.\n+\n+## 7. Route Lock-In\n+\n+- [ ] **Requirements:** Early exploration should be possible, but the game should eventually lock into one romance route. Other Baggers remain available for friendship/support scenes.\n+\n+- [ ] **Requirements:** Lock-in must feel intentional and emotionally significant.\n+\n+- [ ] **Implementation:** Add `lockedRoute` and per-Bagger `commitmentScore`. Repeated dates, confession-adjacent choices, high Gemini romance pressure, and route promises increase commitment.\n+\n+- [ ] **Implementation:** Before irreversible lock-in, show a clear route-commitment scene.\n+\n+- [ ] **Quality Gates:** One casual interaction must not lock the player in.\n+\n+- [ ] **Quality Gates:** Finale scenes must only appear for the locked route.\n+\n+- [ ] **Quality Gates:** Post-lock non-route Bagger scenes should still feel respectful and complete.\n+\n+## 8. Date-Location System\n+\n+- [ ] **Requirements:** Dates should involve selecting a Bagger, a location, and optionally a gift. Each location should have tags and Bagger preferences.\n+\n+- [ ] **Requirements:** Locations should be authored and memorable, not just dropdown labels.\n+\n+- [ ] **Implementation:** Add locations like observatory, old tunnel, riverbed, workshop, night festival, scrapyard, hill road, rain shelter, quarry edge, and maintenance garage.\n+\n+- [ ] **Implementation:** Location tags include `quiet`, `public`, `technical`, `dangerous`, `romantic`, `memory`, `water`, `rescue`, and `stars`. Bagger preferences map to these tags.\n+\n+- [ ] **Quality Gates:** Each Bagger needs preferred, neutral, and disliked locations.\n+\n+- [ ] **Quality Gates:** Good, neutral, and poor dates should produce visibly different outcomes.\n+\n+- [ ] **Quality Gates:** Poor dates should create awkward or repair content, not just punishment.\n+\n+## 9. Gift Economy And Inventory\n+\n+- [ ] **Requirements:** Add inventory, currency, gift shopping, rare finds, route-critical gifts, liked gifts, and disliked gifts.\n+\n+- [ ] **Requirements:** Gifts should carry story meaning and should matter by timing and route context.\n+\n+- [ ] **Implementation:** Add items like `kiesel`, `tool`, `oil`, `star-map`, `rescue-badge`, `river-stone`, `glass-charm`, `lamp-bulb`, and `folded-map`.\n+\n+- [ ] **Implementation:** Gift resolution combines deterministic preference rules with Gemini's emotional interpretation.\n+\n+- [ ] **Quality Gates:** Gifts must be consumed correctly and persist across reloads.\n+\n+- [ ] **Quality Gates:** Each Bagger should have at least 5 liked gifts, 2 disliked gifts, and 1 route-critical gift.\n+\n+- [ ] **Quality Gates:** Normal endings should not become impossible because of one missed shop item.\n+\n+## 10. Hidden Flags And Promise System\n+\n+- [ ] **Requirements:** Track promises made, promises kept, promises broken, missed dates, favorite locations, emotional boundaries, jealousy, accepted gifts, crisis recovery, and secret-route hints.\n+\n+- [ ] **Requirements:** Promises should be an emotional core of the game.\n+\n+- [ ] **Implementation:** Add typed flags: `promise`, `keptPromise`, `brokenPromise`, `memory`, `mistake`, `route`, `secret`, `jealousy`, `boundary`, and `crisis`.\n+\n+- [ ] **Implementation:** Promise flags should include `route`, `createdDay`, `dueDay`, `duePeriod`, `kept`, `broken`, and `sceneId`.\n+\n+- [ ] **Quality Gates:** Every major route event should set or clear at least one meaningful flag.\n+\n+- [ ] **Quality Gates:** Broken promises must affect later dialogue or ending resolution.\n+\n+- [ ] **Quality Gates:** Gemini-suggested flags must be known IDs.\n+\n+## 11. Backlog And Dialogue History\n+\n+- [ ] **Requirements:** Add a VN-style backlog showing narration, player choices, Bagger dialogue, day, period, location, route, and speaker.\n+\n+- [ ] **Requirements:** Backlog is literal transcript. Memories are summarized emotional facts. They must remain separate.\n+\n+- [ ] **Implementation:** Add `state.backlog` entries like `{ type, speaker, text, day, period, location, route, choiceId }`.\n+\n+- [ ] **Implementation:** Add a backlog panel with route filtering and a cap around 300 entries.\n+\n+- [ ] **Quality Gates:** Backlog must persist across reloads.\n+\n+- [ ] **Quality Gates:** Backlog must not include API keys, raw prompts, or backend debug details.\n+\n+- [ ] **Quality Gates:** Every visible dialogue should appear in backlog.\n+\n+## 12. Presentation Polish\n+\n+- [ ] **Requirements:** Add route title cards, fades, dramatic pauses, choice reveal animations, affection-change feedback, scene transition text, and expressive 3D Bagger poses.\n+\n+- [ ] **Requirements:** Presentation should support emotional pacing.\n+\n+- [ ] **Implementation:** Create a scene presentation layer with `showNarration`, `showDialogue`, `showChoices`, `transitionToLocation`, `showStatChange`, and `applyPose`.\n+\n+- [ ] **Implementation:** 3D model poses should include listening, shy, proud, guarded, digging, crisis, and confession.\n+\n+- [ ] **Quality Gates:** Animations must respect reduced-motion settings.\n+\n+- [ ] **Quality Gates:** WebGL failure should still leave the game playable.\n+\n+- [ ] **Quality Gates:** Effects must not cause double submissions or broken state updates.\n+\n+## 13. Failure Pressure And Missed Opportunities\n+\n+- [ ] **Requirements:** Ignoring Baggers, missing promised dates, overworking, incompatible actions, or reaching Day 30 without commitment should have consequences.\n+\n+- [ ] **Requirements:** Failure should create drama, not random punishment.\n+\n+- [ ] **Implementation:** Track `neglect`, `missedPromises`, `fatigue`, `routeDeadlineMisses`, and `unresolvedCrisis`.\n+\n+- [ ] **Implementation:** Add warning scenes before severe consequences.\n+\n+- [ ] **Quality Gates:** A neglectful playthrough should reliably produce missed-route or friendship endings.\n+\n+- [ ] **Quality Gates:** One bad choice should not destroy a strong route.\n+\n+- [ ] **Quality Gates:** Repair branches should exist after some mistakes.\n+\n+## 14. Protagonist Setup\n+\n+- [ ] **Requirements:** At new game start, the player chooses name, address style, and personality inclination: earnest, shy, bold, practical, or poetic.\n+\n+- [ ] **Requirements:** The protagonist should feel present but flexible.\n+\n+- [ ] **Implementation:** Add a start screen if no profile exists. Store `player.name`, `player.address`, `player.style`, and starting stat bonuses.\n+\n+- [ ] **Implementation:** Personality affects starting stats and Gemini tone, but does not hard-lock choices.\n+\n+- [ ] **Quality Gates:** Names must be sanitized and length-limited.\n+\n+- [ ] **Quality Gates:** Gemini must not overwrite profile data.\n+\n+- [ ] **Quality Gates:** Profile setup should happen once per new game unless deliberately reset.\n+\n+## 15. Server-Side Persistence\n+\n+- [ ] **Requirements:** Add durable server-side persistence without adding save slots. Use one anonymous save token that can restore progress on another device.\n+\n+- [ ] **Requirements:** Saves include route state, flags, inventory, capped backlog, player profile, and schema version.\n+\n+- [ ] **Implementation:** Add endpoints like `POST /api/save`, `GET /api/save/:token`, and `POST /api/restore`.\n+\n+- [ ] **Implementation:** Store JSON saves under `/opt/bagger-api/data/saves` or use SQLite with schema versioning and atomic writes.\n+\n+- [ ] **Quality Gates:** Saves must be atomic, size-limited, schema-versioned, and recoverable after service restart.\n+\n+- [ ] **Quality Gates:** Invalid tokens and corrupted saves must fail safely.\n+\n+- [ ] **Quality Gates:** No API key, raw Gemini prompt, or sensitive server detail should be stored.\n+\n+## 16. Gemini-Guided Progression\n+\n+- [ ] **Requirements:** Gemini should influence progression meaningfully. It should interpret player wording, selected choices, route state, memories, and context to propose stat deltas, flags, route pressure, scene tone, and memory.\n+\n+- [ ] **Requirements:** We do not add custom safety gates beyond Gemini's built-in behavior. We only perform operational validation for save integrity.\n+\n+- [ ] **Implementation:** Change `/api/interact` so Gemini returns structured data: `reply`, `emotionalRead`, `bondDelta`, `trustDelta`, `warmthDelta`, `depthDelta`, `courageDelta`, `suggestedFlags`, `routePressure`, `nextTone`, `memory`, and `visual`.\n+\n+- [ ] **Implementation:** The engine clamps deltas to scene-specific ranges and accepts only known flags, but Gemini's interpretation materially affects stats and route direction.\n+\n+- [ ] **Implementation:** Route pressure categories include `toward_romance`, `toward_friendship`, `needs_repair`, `opens_secret`, `toward_crisis`, and `toward_lockin`.\n+\n+- [ ] **Quality Gates:** The same menu choice with different text should produce different outcomes.\n+\n+- [ ] **Quality Gates:** Gemini-suggested flags must be from the known route registry.\n+\n+- [ ] **Quality Gates:** If Gemini fails, preserve state or use a neutral fallback without corrupting progression.\n+\n+## 17. Debug Tooling And Route Simulation\n+\n+- [ ] **Requirements:** This project needs developer-facing debug tooling because route graphs, flags, Gemini progression, calendar events, and endings will become complex. The tooling can be CLI, API, or both. It should be private/local by default.\n+\n+- [ ] **Requirements:** The tooling should answer practical questions: why is a scene locked, which endings are reachable, which flags were set, what Gemini proposed, which deltas were clamped, what route pressure changed, and what event triggers next.\n+\n+- [ ] **Implementation:** Add a CLI tool such as `/opt/bagger-api/tools/simulate.py` with commands: `list-routes`, `show-route aurora`, `validate-routes`, `simulate --route aurora --strategy romance`, `simulate --route mira --strategy neglect`, `ending-fixture true_aurora`, and `explain-scene --save token --scene aurora_observatory_2`.\n+\n+- [ ] **Implementation:** Add private API endpoints bound to localhost or protected by `BAGGER_DEBUG=1`: `GET /api/debug/state/:token`, `POST /api/debug/explain-scene`, `GET /api/debug/routes`, `POST /api/debug/simulate`, and `POST /api/debug/resolve-ending`.\n+\n+- [ ] **Implementation:** Add debug traces for each scene resolution: selected scene, candidate scenes, rejected scenes with reasons, Gemini emotional read, proposed deltas, clamped deltas, accepted flags, rejected flags, route pressure changes, calendar event consumed, time advanced, and ending checks.\n+\n+- [ ] **Quality Gates:** `validate-routes` should fail if scenes reference unknown flags, impossible gates, missing next nodes, missing style notes, missing motifs, unknown locations, or unreachable endings.\n+\n+- [ ] **Quality Gates:** Simulations should cover `romance`, `friendship`, `balanced`, `neglect`, `gift-heavy`, and `training-heavy`.\n+\n+- [ ] **Quality Gates:** Debug endpoints must not expose Gemini API keys, raw prompts with sensitive data, or unrestricted file access. Public Caddy routes should not expose debug APIs.\n+\n+## Build Phases\n+\n+- [ ] **Phase 1:** Style bible, state schema, route-definition format, and debug validation scaffolding.\n+\n+- [ ] **Phase 2:** Route graph resolver, calendar events, scheduling, stat gates, and route lock-in.\n+\n+- [ ] **Phase 3:** Gemini-guided structured progression with stat deltas, flags, route pressure, and style-bible prompt compression.\n+\n+- [ ] **Phase 4:** Dates, gifts, inventory, promises, failure pressure, and endings.\n+\n+- [ ] **Phase 5:** Protagonist setup, backlog, presentation polish, server-side persistence, and WebGL fallback refinement.\n+\n+- [ ] **Phase 6:** Full content pass, route writing, simulation fixtures, ending verification, mobile testing, service restart testing, and production verification.\n*** End Patch"
}

Your goal is to implement the complete plan including all 17 things AND all 6 phases + testing + polish. gpt-5.5 | agent=build | finish=tool-calls
▶ Reasoning
**Considering frontend-backend interaction**

I’m thinking about how to streamline scene selection by having the frontend only handle certain uses. It would be simpler if the frontend sends state and intent to the backend's `/api/interact`, which then resolves the scene, interacts with Gemini, and returns a new state patch. However, the frontend still needs to manage scheduling, dates, and gifts. To implement this fully, it seems best if the backend defines choices, while the frontend retrieves options. An alternative could be having the frontend as the game engine with the backend focusing on Gemini and data persistence.
Ich setze das jetzt als echten Build um, nicht nur als UI-Update: zuerst Daten-/Engine-Struktur und Backend-APIs, dann Frontend für die vollständigen Systeme, dann Debug-CLI und Tests. Ich gehe entlang der 17 Schritte, damit nichts aus dem Plan verloren geht.

▶ Reasoning
**Considering save endpoints**

I'm thinking about how save endpoints work. It seems like I should store the whole state from the client because that way, the API can return a result without mutating the full state. That might simplify things. Then, the frontend will just need to apply the returned state. Overall, this approach could streamline the communication between the client and the server while preserving the integrity of the data. Let's see how this plays out!
Ich lege die Spieldefinitionen serverseitig an: Style Bible, 60+ Route-Nodes, Locations, Items, Kalender, Schedule-Aktionen, Endings, Flag-Registry und Debug-/Simulationsgrundlagen. Damit werden Routen, Gates und Enden nicht mehr im Frontend versteckt.

{
  "patchText": "*** Begin Patch\n*** Add File: /opt/bagger-api/game_data.py\n+STYLE_BIBLE = {\n+    \"global\": {\n+        \"target\": \"sincere Japanese visual novel localized into English, rendered in German-facing UI\",\n+        \"rules\": [\n+            \"ADV textbox style: compact paragraphs, direct emotional pressure, no markdown.\",\n+            \"Show feeling through machine behavior, weather, objects, pauses, and repeated motifs.\",\n+            \"Never mention stats, meters, route pressure, flags, or game mechanics in character dialogue.\",\n+            \"Avoid therapy-chat and generic AI romance. Use concrete sensory details.\",\n+            \"Keep romantic tone safe, earnest, all-ages, restrained, and emotionally specific.\",\n+        ],\n+        \"scene_rhythm\": \"opening image, small mechanical reaction, emotional turn, lingering object or silence\",\n+        \"bad_phrases\": [\n+            \"our bond grows stronger\",\n+            \"I feel a deep connection\",\n+            \"you make my heart race\",\n+            \"this means so much to me\",\n+            \"I am here to support you\",\n+            \"as an AI\",\n+            \"deeply connected on an emotional level\",\n+        ],\n+        \"better_examples\": [\n+            \"The warning lamp blinked once, then stayed lit.\",\n+            \"My bucket stopped before it touched the mud.\",\n+            \"I kept the small stone in the dry part of my cab.\",\n+            \"The tunnel did not echo as loudly when you stood there.\",\n+        ],\n+    },\n+    \"routes\": {\n+        \"aurora\": {\n+            \"motifs\": [\"moonlight\", \"glass\", \"puddles\", \"old star maps\", \"observatory dust\", \"quiet hydraulics\", \"lens reflections\"],\n+            \"voice\": \"careful, elliptical, slow to trust, frightened by being left behind\",\n+            \"avoid\": \"sudden declarations; Brummbert withdraws from careless bravado\",\n+        },\n+        \"mira\": {\n+            \"motifs\": [\"river stones\", \"sediment lines\", \"folded maps\", \"clay\", \"underground springs\", \"hidden water\"],\n+            \"voice\": \"poetic but not purple, precise, distant until trust becomes chosen intimacy\",\n+            \"avoid\": \"overexplaining poetry; Jetzt muss er lernen, dass auch Retter gehalten werden duerfen.\",\n+        \"colors\": {\"body\": 0xf36d3d, \"accent\": 0xffd166, \"dark\": 0x17100c},\n+        \"preferredTags\": [\"rescue\", \"technical\", \"warm\", \"quiet\"],\n+        \"dislikedTags\": [\"mocking\", \"reckless\", \"cold\"],\n+    },\n+    \"mira\": {\n+        \"name\": \"Mira Schaufelstern\",\n+        \"soul\": \"eine traeumerische Tiefenpoetin, die unterirdische Fluesse kartiert und Gefuehle in Erdschichten liest\",\n+        \"routeWord\": \"Tiefenpoesie-Route\",\n+        \"story\": \"Sie liest Lehm wie andere Briefe lesen.
